evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:166-210 — execute_remediation returns a hardcoded dict with string literals such as 'Analyzed pod events and logs' and 'Would restart pod with corrected image'; no kubernetes client import exists in the file (only asyncio, json, typing, config, logger are imported at lines 5-10)
diagnosis: execute_remediation never calls any real Kubernetes API; it returns fabricated action strings based on string-matching the issue_type parameter, giving callers false confidence that remediation occurred.
proposed_fix: Add 'from kubernetes import client, config as k8s_config' import. In initialize(), call k8s_config.load_incluster_config() or load_kube_config(). In execute_remediation(), for pod_restart_loop call CoreV1Api().delete_namespaced_pod(); for node_not_ready exec kubelet restart via CoreV1Api().connect_get_namespaced_pod_exec(). Propagate dry_run flag to each API call. Return actual API responses, not strings.
test_plan: Unit test with mocked kubernetes.client: assert delete_namespaced_pod is called with correct pod/namespace when dry_run=False and NOT called when dry_run=True. Integration test against a kind cluster: introduce a crashloop pod, call execute_remediation, assert pod restarts within 30s.

evidence: No file in src/ implements a review->PRD->split->implement->verify loop or any state machine. The mcp_server.py dispatch table (lines 489-610) handles single-shot tool calls only.
diagnosis: There is no iteration state machine design anywhere in the codebase; the behavior must be designed from scratch for the auto-remediator PRD.
proposed_fix: Data model: RemediationSession(id, state: Enum[REVIEW|PRD|SPLIT|IMPLEMENT|VERIFY|DONE|FAILED], issue_snapshot, plan_steps: List[Step], current_step_idx, attempts, created_at, updated_at). Interface: async def advance(session: RemediationSession) -> RemediationSession. Stop conditions: state==DONE (all steps verified), state==FAILED (attempts > max_retries or verify detects regression), timeout exceeded. Persist sessions in SQLite. Each state transition is an idempotent async function callable independently for resumability.
test_plan: Unit test each state transition function in isolation with mocked k8s client. Integration test: inject a known issue, run the state machine to completion, assert final state is DONE and cluster health check passes. Test FAILED path by injecting an irrecoverable condition and asserting max_retries stops the loop.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:91-163 — get_recommendations returns hardcoded recommendation dicts (e.g. line 116: 'title': 'Optimize Resource Requests and Limits') constructed purely from the focus_areas list; no kubernetes client import exists (lines 5-10), so no actual cluster state is read.
diagnosis: get_recommendations produces static, generic advice independent of actual cluster state; the cluster_context parameter is accepted but completely ignored, and no real API calls are made.
proposed_fix: Initialize kubernetes client in KubernetesManager.initialize(). In get_recommendations(), call AppsV1Api().list_deployment_for_all_namespaces() to find deployments missing resource requests, AutoscalingV1Api().list_horizontal_pod_autoscaler_for_all_namespaces() to find unscaled workloads, and NetworkingV1Api().list_network_policy_for_all_namespaces() for segmentation gaps. Build recommendations from actual API findings, not static strings.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes API responses with one deployment missing resources and one without HPA. Assert returned recommendations reference the actual deployment names and namespaces. Assert empty cluster returns empty recommendation lists, not generic static items.

evidence: setup-vscode-k8s.sh:77 — hardcoded path '/Users/hawaiidevelopergmail.com/Library/Application Support/Code/User/settings.json' (missing '@' in email-derived path fragment); line 89 repeats same broken path; line 8 correctly uses $HOME but the embedded Python heredoc ignores it.
diagnosis: The embedded Python script uses a hardcoded incorrect filesystem path instead of the shell variable SETTINGS_FILE, so the script will always fail with FileNotFoundError on any machine other than the one it was developed on (and even there, the path is malformed).
proposed_fix: Pass SETTINGS_FILE as an argument or environment variable into the Python heredoc: python3 -c "import json, os, sys; sf=os.environ['SETTINGS_FILE']; ..." with SETTINGS_FILE="$SETTINGS_FILE" in the environment. Remove the hardcoded path. Also add 'set -euo pipefail' before line 1 (it is present at line 3 but only after the shebang; confirm it is line 3 not after sourced code).
test_plan: Run the script on a test machine where $HOME differs from the hardcoded value. Assert the script reads and writes $SETTINGS_FILE not the hardcoded path. Run with shellcheck to confirm no unquoted variables or path issues.

evidence: No watchdog implementation exists anywhere in src/. The mcp_server.py handles one-shot tool calls (lines 489-610) with no background loop, timer, or health-gate logic.
diagnosis: The five-minute watchdog behavior must be designed from scratch; the PRD should define the heal-detection contract precisely.
proposed_fix: Implement as an asyncio task: async def watchdog_loop(session, interval=300). After each remediation step, poll: (1) target pod readiness via CoreV1Api().read_namespaced_pod_status() — all containers must be Ready==True; (2) Event stream must show zero new Warning events for the target resource in the last 60s (CoreV1Api().list_namespaced_event with fieldSelector=involvedObject.name). Declare healed only when both conditions hold for two consecutive polls. On timeout (5 min), mark session FAILED. Emit a structured log entry with evidence for DPO extraction.
test_plan: Unit test: mock pod status returning Ready=False then Ready=True; assert watchdog emits healed=True only after the second Ready=True poll. Integration test: deploy a crashloop pod, trigger remediation, assert watchdog resolves within 5 min on a kind cluster. Test timeout path: assert FAILED is emitted when pod never becomes ready.

evidence: README.md:58 — 'Production Ready: Successfully deployed in both Docker containers and Kubernetes clusters'; README.md:204 — 'Live Issue Database — Monitors 17 major Kubernetes repositories with 45,720+ indexed issues'; README.md:113-133 — lists specific live cluster IP 172.100.10.107:30001 and claims '1/1 replicas ready'. evidence: No DPO extraction code exists in src/. The github_issues_manager.py exists but contains no pair-generation or issue-body emission logic for training data.
diagnosis: The DPO pair extraction behavior is entirely unimplemented and must be designed for the PRD.
proposed_fix: Data model: DPOPair(session_id, prompt: str, chosen_action: RemediationStep, rejected_actions: List[RemediationStep], evidence: Dict[str, Any]). Interface: def extract_dpo_pairs(session: RemediationSession) -> List[DPOPair]. Implementation: for each step where alternatives were evaluated, the taken path (healed=True) is chosen; untaken paths or paths that led to regression are rejected. Emit as GitHub issue body with sections: ## Prompt, ## Chosen, ## Rejected (list), ## Evidence (API response snippets). Gate extraction on session.state==DONE to avoid partial data.
test_plan: Unit test: build a synthetic RemediationSession with two steps, one with alternatives; assert extract_dpo_pairs returns exactly one pair with correct chosen/rejected split. Integration test: run a full session on kind cluster and assert the emitted issue body parses as valid JSON DPO format.

evidence: setup-vscode-nodeport.sh:13-14 — CLUSTER_IP and NODEPORT are set by running kubectl commands before set -euo pipefail takes effect (set -euo pipefail is at line 6, but kubectl at lines 13-14 runs without checking if kubectl is available or if the cluster is reachable); line 50 — heredoc uses unquoted EOF (EOF not 'EOF') so $CLUSTER_IP and $NODEPORT expand inside the heredoc, injecting shell-variable values directly into the JSON written to /tmp/mcp_nodeport_config.json, creating a path injection risk if CLUSTER_IP contains special characters.
diagnosis: The NodePort script does not validate that CLUSTER_IP and NODEPORT are non-empty before using them in a JSON config file written to /tmp, and the unquoted heredoc allows variable expansion that could corrupt the JSON if the cluster IP contains unexpected characters.
proposed_fix: After lines 13-14 add: [[ -z "$CLUSTER_IP" ]] && { echo 'ERROR: could not determine cluster IP'; exit 1; }. Validate NODEPORT is numeric: [[ "$NODEPORT" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || { echo 'ERROR: invalid nodeport'; exit 1; }. Use printf '%s' to write JSON safely rather than an unquoted heredoc. Write config to a temp file with mktemp rather than a predictable /tmp path.
test_plan: Run with no cluster available and assert non-zero exit with clear error. Run with a mocked CLUSTER_IP containing a double-quote character and assert the script exits with error rather than writing malformed JSON.

evidence: stop.sh:1-5 — no shebang is technically #!/bin/bash at line 1, set -euo pipefail is absent; the '|| true' on lines 3-4 swallows all errors silently including unexpected ones; the script does not verify Docker is installed before calling docker stop.
diagnosis: stop.sh lacks set -euo pipefail and performs no error distinction, so a missing Docker binary or daemon crash produces the same silent success output as a clean stop, making automated healthcheck pipelines unable to detect failures.
proposed_fix: Add 'set -euo pipefail' after shebang. Replace '|| true' with explicit checks: 'docker stop k8s-mcp-server 2>/dev/null; ret=$?; if [[ $ret -ne 0 && $ret -ne 1 ]]; then echo "ERROR: docker stop failed ($ret)"; exit $ret; fi'. Check 'command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "docker not found"; exit 1; }' before any docker call.
test_plan: Run stop.sh when Docker daemon is not running; assert non-zero exit code. Run stop.sh when container does not exist; assert exit 0 (idempotent). Run stop.sh when container is running; assert container is stopped and removed.

evidence: security-scan-demo.sh:1 — #!/bin/bash (no set -euo pipefail); security-scan-demo.sh:150 — hardcoded conclusion '✅ Your MCP server follows security best practices!'
diagnosis: Script lacks set -euo pipefail so any kubectl or jq failure is silently swallowed, and it unconditionally emits a passing verdict regardless of scan results.
proposed_fix: Add 'set -euo pipefail' on line 2. Replace the hardcoded line 150 with a computed EXIT_CODE that is non-zero when any check emits WARNING or CRITICAL, and replace the hardcoded praise with a conditional summary: 'if [ "$ISSUES" -gt 0 ]; then echo "FAIL: $ISSUES issues found"; exit 1; else echo "PASS"; fi'.
test_plan: Run script against a cluster with a known privileged pod; verify exit code is non-zero and no 'best practices' praise is printed. Run against a clean cluster; verify exit 0 and PASS message.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:65-74 — single KubernetesManager, KubectlManager, HelmManager all instantiated with same config; no ServiceAccount separation exists anywhere in src/
diagnosis: There is no RBAC split implemented: all operations — read-only diagnosis and write/apply — share one identity with no least-privilege separation.

Interface: KubernetesManager.get_read_client() / get_write_client() return separate kubernetes.client.ApiClient instances loaded from respective kubeconfigs. Dispatch: mcp_server.py routes all 'get_*', 'diagnose_*', 'search_*' tools to read_client; 'execute_remediation', 'kubectl_apply', 'helm_install' to write_client. Edge cases: write_client raises PermissionError if namespace is in PROTECTED_NAMESPACES list (ibkr-live-trader, daxxon-trading, brightflow-live).

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:60-61 — 'self.non_destructive_mode = os.getenv("ALLOW_ONLY_NON_DESTRUCTIVE_TOOLS", "false").lower() == "true"'; no mention of ibkr-live-trader, daxxon-trading, or brightflow-live anywhere in src/

Integration test: deploy to cluster with non_destructive_mode=false; verify apply to ibkr-live-trader is blocked with audit-log entry.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:487 — 'self.logger.info(f"Tool called: {name} with arguments: {arguments}")'; no structured audit log, no who/what/when/evidence record, no persistent log store referenced anywhere in src/
diagnosis: All write actions are logged only to the application logger at INFO level; there is no tamper-evident, persistent audit log recording actor identity, action taken, target resource, evidence used, and outcome.

Wiring: call AuditLogger.record() in handle_call_tool after every tool invocation, capturing the outcome and any exception message as evidence. evidence: No SQLite worklist exists anywhere in the repo (grep -r sqlite returns zero results); concurrent agent claims are unguarded.
diagnosis: There is no atomic claim/release mechanism for parallel remediation agents, making it possible for two agents to remediate the same resource simultaneously.
proposed_fix: Create src/worklist.py with a WorklistDB class backed by SQLite WAL mode. Schema: `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS claims (item_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, claimed_by TEXT NOT NULL, claimed_at REAL NOT NULL, ttl_seconds INTEGER DEFAULT 300)`. Expose `claim(item_id, agent_id) -> bool` (INSERT OR IGNORE, check rowcount==1) and `release(item_id, agent_id)` (DELETE WHERE item_id=? AND claimed_by=?). Add `reap_expired()` called on every claim to remove stale locks. Use `sqlite3` with `isolation_level=None` (autocommit) and `check_same_thread=False` guarded by a threading.Lock for thread safety.
test_plan: Unit test: two threads call claim() for the same item_id; assert exactly one returns True. Integration test: claim, simulate crash (no release), advance time past TTL, call reap_expired(), assert item is claimable again.

evidence: No remediation state machine exists in the repo; src/k8s_manager.py execute_remediation returns hardcoded stub data (confirmed by audit context).
diagnosis: No rollout-restart escalation ladder is implemented; the design for a staged remediation state machine (restart -> wait -> escalate) does not exist.
proposed_fix: Design a RemediationStateMachine with states: IDLE -> RESTART_ISSUED -> WAITING (5 min timer) -> VERIFY -> ESCALATED -> RESOLVED. Data model: `{item_id, resource_kind, resource_name, namespace, state, restart_ts, verify_ts, escalation_level}` stored in SQLite via worklist. Interface: `async def advance(item_id) -> RemediationState`. On IDLE: call `kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name> -n <ns>`; transition to RESTART_ISSUED. On RESTART_ISSUED after 5 min: poll `kubectl rollout status`; if successful -> RESOLVED, if still failing -> ESCALATED. ESCALATED triggers alert via configurable webhook. Edge cases: resource deleted mid-ladder (catch ApiException 404 -> RESOLVED), cluster unreachable (retry with backoff, max 3).
test_plan: Unit-test each state transition with mocked kubectl calls. Integration test: deploy a bad image, trigger ladder, assert rollout restart is called, then assert escalation fires when rollout does not converge within timeout.

evidence: setup-vscode-nodeport.sh:1 — no shebang present in first visible line; file starts with `#!/bin/bash` (line 1 is correct) but CLUSTER_IP/NODEPORT are derived from live kubectl at lines 13-14 with no error checking if commands fail; `set -euo pipefail` is present at line 6 which is good, but `CLUSTER_IP` and `NODEPORT` can expand to empty strings silently if kubectl returns empty jsonpath, causing `http://:0/health` URL construction
diagnosis: CLUSTER_IP and NODEPORT are set from kubectl jsonpath with no guard against empty output, so the script silently constructs broken URLs and continues instead of failing fast.
proposed_fix: After lines 13-14, add: `[[ -z "$CLUSTER_IP" ]] && { echo 'ERROR: Could not determine cluster IP'; exit 1; }` and `[[ -z "$NODEPORT" ]] && { echo 'ERROR: Could not determine NodePort'; exit 1; }`. Also validate NODEPORT is numeric: `[[ "$NODEPORT" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || { echo 'ERROR: Invalid NodePort value'; exit 1; }`.
test_plan: Run the script against a cluster where the NodePort service does not exist; assert it exits non-zero with a clear error message rather than hitting an invalid URL.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:229-257 — Tool(name='kubectl_rollout', ...) defined with actions [status, history, undo, restart]. src/mcp_server.py has no import of enhanced_tools and no dispatch branch for 'kubectl_rollout' (confirmed by grep returning zero matches for 'kubectl_rollout' in mcp_server.py).
diagnosis: kubectl_rollout Tool schema is defined but never registered or dispatched; calling it from an MCP client would hit the 'Unknown tool' branch and return an error.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py handle_call_tool(), add: `elif name == 'kubectl_rollout': result = await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, self.kubectl_manager.rollout, arguments['action'], arguments['resource'], arguments['name'], arguments.get('namespace'))`. Implement KubectlManager.rollout() to call `_run_kubectl(['rollout', action, f'{resource}/{name}'] + ns_args)`. Register the tool by including get_enhanced_kubectl_tools() in handle_list_tools().
test_plan: Mock _run_kubectl; call handle_call_tool with name='kubectl_rollout' and action='status'; assert _run_kubectl is called with ['rollout','status','deployment/myapp'] and result is returned rather than 'Unknown tool'.

evidence: logs.sh:1-3 — file is 3 lines: `#!/bin/bash`, `echo ...`, `docker logs k8s-mcp-server -f`. No `set -euo pipefail`. If the container does not exist, docker logs exits non-zero but the script has no error handling and will not inform the user clearly.
diagnosis: logs.sh lacks error hardening (no set -euo pipefail, no container-existence check) and hard-codes the container name k8s-mcp-server which may differ from the actual deployed container name.
proposed_fix:
Replace with: `#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
CONTAINER=${K8S_MCP_CONTAINER:-k8s-mcp-server}
if ! docker inspect "$CONTAINER" >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "ERROR: container $CONTAINER not found"; exit 1; fi
docker logs "$CONTAINER" -f`
test_plan: Run the script when no Docker container exists; assert exit code is non-zero and error message is printed. Run when container exists; assert docker logs is called with correct name.

evidence: src/logger.py:173 — `logger.bind(...).error(f'K8s Error...', exc_info=error)` and src/logger.py:181 — same pattern. Loguru's `.error()` does not accept an `exc_info` keyword argument; that argument belongs to stdlib logging. Loguru uses `.opt(exception=error).error()`.
diagnosis: log_k8s_error() and log_diagnostic_error() pass exc_info=error as a keyword to loguru's .error(), which silently ignores it, meaning stack traces are never captured in error logs.
proposed_fix: Replace both error calls: change `logger.bind(...).error(msg, exc_info=error)` to `logger.bind(...).opt(exception=error).error(msg)`. Same fix for log_diagnostic_error at line 180. Also remove the unused `from rich.console import Console` import at line 11 and the unused `console = Console()` at line 39 (Console is constructed but never used in setup_logging).
test_plan: Call log_k8s_error() with a live Exception; assert the log output contains a traceback. Run `python -c 'from logger import log_k8s_error; log_k8s_error("test", ValueError("x"))'` and assert no TypeError is raised.

evidence: setup-vscode-k8s.sh:77 — `with open("/Users/hawaiidevelopergmail.com/Library/Application Support/Code/User/settings.json", 'r')` and line 89 same path. The Python heredoc uses a hardcoded path with a mangled username (`hawaiidevelopergmail.com` instead of `hawaiideveloper`) and ignores the `$SETTINGS_FILE` variable defined at line 9.
diagnosis: The Python heredoc uses a single-quoted EOF marker (line 72: `<< 'EOF'`), which prevents variable expansion, so $SETTINGS_FILE is never substituted and a wrong hardcoded path is used instead, causing FileNotFoundError for every user.
proposed_fix: Change `python3 << 'EOF'` to `python3 << EOF` so that `$SETTINGS_FILE` expands, then replace both hardcoded path strings in the heredoc with `$SETTINGS_FILE`. Alternatively, pass SETTINGS_FILE as an env var: `SETTINGS_FILE="$SETTINGS_FILE" python3 -c 'import os,json; ...` reading from `os.environ["SETTINGS_FILE"]`.
test_plan: Run the script as a non-root user whose home directory is not /Users/hawaiidevelopergmail.com; assert the settings.json is read from and written to $HOME/Library/Application Support/Code/User/settings.json.

evidence: src/__init__.py:1-8 — declares `__version__ = '1.0.0'` and `__author__ = 'Kubernetes Platform Engineer'`. No imports of sub-modules, no public API surface exported. The file is a passive namespace marker.
diagnosis: __init__.py exports a static version string but does not expose any public API, making the src package unusable as a library and providing no version-sync with setup.py/pyproject.toml if one is added.
proposed_fix: Either leave as-is with a comment explaining it is intentionally minimal, or add `from .mcp_server import KubernetesPlatformEngineerMCPServer` and `__all__ = ['KubernetesPlatformEngineerMCPServer']` to give the package a usable public entry point. If a pyproject.toml is added later, version should be read via `importlib.metadata.version('mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer')` rather than hardcoded.
test_plan: Import the src package and assert `src.__version__` equals the version in pyproject.toml/setup.cfg. If public exports are added, assert `from src import KubernetesPlatformEngineerMCPServer` succeeds without error.

evidence: No event stream watcher exists in the repo (grep -r 'watch\|event_stream\|EventWatcher' src/ returns no relevant matches). The monitoring_manager is a stub returning canned data.
diagnosis: No implementation or design exists for watching the Kubernetes event stream, classifying events, or routing them to a remediation queue.
proposed_fix: Design an EventStreamWatcher class using `kubernetes.watch.Watch().stream(core_v1.list_event_for_all_namespaces)`. Data model: `KubernetesEvent {type, reason, message, involved_object_kind, involved_object_name, namespace, count, first_time, last_time}`. Classification rules: map (reason, involvedObject.kind) -> EventClass enum (POD_CRASH_LOOP, OOM_KILL, IMAGE_PULL_ERROR, NODE_NOT_READY, PVC_PENDING). Routing: push classified events to an asyncio.Queue consumed by the RemediationStateMachine. Interface: `async def run()` (infinite loop with reconnect on ApiException). Edge cases: duplicate event suppression via (name, reason, count) dedup window of 60s; graceful shutdown via asyncio.Event; reconnect backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s).
test_plan: Unit-test classification rules with fixture events for each EventClass. Integration test: mock Watch().stream() to emit a CrashLoopBackOff event; assert it appears in the remediation queue with correct classification within 1 second.

evidence: design-task — no existing dedup logic found in any manager or server file
diagnosis: No deduplication mechanism exists; the PRD must define how identical root-cause findings emitted for N resources are collapsed into a single finding with an affected-resource list.
proposed_fix: Add a FindingDeduplicator class: key = (check_type, issue_reason, normalized_message_template). Each unique key maps to a FindingGroup(root_cause, affected_resources: List[str], count, first_seen, last_seen). Deduplication runs after all check_* methods return, before results are serialized. Edge cases: same reason/different namespaces should be grouped separately; transient vs persistent states need a stability window (e.g., >=2 observations) before grouping.
test_plan: Unit test: inject 5 pod objects with ImagePullBackOff across 2 namespaces; assert output contains exactly 2 FindingGroups with counts 3 and 2. Integration test: run diagnose_cluster_health against a live cluster with known duplicates and assert the response payload has no duplicate (check_type, reason) pairs.

evidence: mcp-port-forward.sh:60 — `sleep 3` and mcp-port-forward.sh:119 — `sleep 2`; mcp-port-forward.sh:100-101 — curl called twice for the same URL; mcp-port-forward.sh:10-11 — LOCAL_PORT and REMOTE_PORT both hardcoded to 3001 with no env-var override
diagnosis: Script has unconditional fixed sleeps instead of polling for readiness, double-invokes curl in test_connectivity leaking any response body to stdout before the conditional check, and exposes no way to change ports without editing the file.
proposed_fix: Replace sleep 3 with a poll loop: `for i in $(seq 1 15); do curl -sf http://localhost:$LOCAL_PORT/health && break; sleep 1; done`. Capture curl once: `RESPONSE=$(curl -sf http://localhost:$LOCAL_PORT/health) && echo "healthy: $RESPONSE"`. Add `LOCAL_PORT=${LOCAL_PORT:-3001}` and `REMOTE_PORT=${REMOTE_PORT:-3001}` at the top so callers can override without editing the file.
test_plan: Run `LOCAL_PORT=3002 ./mcp-port-forward.sh start` against a mock kubectl stub and verify it uses 3002. Confirm test_connectivity emits exactly one curl call by tracing with bash -x and asserting no duplicate HTTP calls.

evidence: design-task — src/diagnostics_manager.py:266-275 _check_storage returns hardcoded stub data; no PVC-specific analyzer class exists anywhere in src/
diagnosis: No PVCAnalyzer exists; the PRD must design one that covers Pending (no matching PV or StorageClass), mount-failed (kubelet attach/mount errors in events), and capacity-exceeded states.
proposed_fix: PVCAnalyzer.analyze(namespace=None) -> List[PVCFinding]: (1) Call core_v1.list_persistent_volume_claim_for_all_namespaces (filtered by namespace if given). (2) For phase==Pending: check StorageClass existence and available PVs matching access-mode and capacity; emit FINDING_PVC_PENDING. (3) For Bound PVCs: query events for 'FailedMount'/'FailedAttach' within last 1h; emit FINDING_PVC_MOUNT_FAILED with node and volume details. (4) For Bound PVCs: compare requests.storage vs capacity.storage; if >90% emit FINDING_PVC_CAPACITY_HIGH. Return typed PVCFinding(pvc_name, namespace, kind, severity, detail).
test_plan: Unit test with mocked k8s API: inject one Pending PVC with missing StorageClass, one Bound PVC with FailedMount event, one Bound PVC at 95% capacity — assert three distinct findings with correct kind values. Integration test: deploy a PVC with a nonexistent storage class and verify FINDING_PVC_PENDING is returned within 5 seconds.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:494-498 dispatches to diagnostics_manager.diagnose_cluster_health; src/diagnostics_manager.py:55 comment 'Stub implementation for different check types'; src/diagnostics_manager.py:217-275 — _check_nodes, _check_pods, _check_services, _check_networking, _check_storage all return hardcoded dictionaries with invented node names, pod counts, and IP addresses, zero calls to kubernetes client
diagnosis: diagnose_cluster_health is fully stubbed and returns invented data regardless of actual cluster state; no kubernetes API is called anywhere in the call chain.
proposed_fix: Inject a kubernetes.client CoreV1Api and AppsV1Api into DiagnosticsManager.__init__. In _check_nodes call core_v1.list_node() and map .status.conditions to ready/not-ready counts. In _check_pods call core_v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces(namespace=namespace) and group by phase. In _check_services call core_v1.list_service_for_all_namespaces. Remove all hardcoded return dicts and populate results from live API responses.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes.client with a fixture that returns two nodes (one NotReady) and verify _check_nodes returns not_ready_nodes=1. Run end-to-end against a real cluster with a deliberately failed pod and assert it appears in issues_found.

evidence: design-task — no allowlist, namespace gating, or PR-only safeguard exists in any source file; execute_remediation at src/mcp_server.py:552-557 dispatches without namespace checks
diagnosis: The PRD must define an allowlisted-namespace mechanism so the auto-remediator applies changes directly only to approved namespaces and raises a PR for all others.
proposed_fix: Add ALLOWED_AUTO_REMEDIATE_NAMESPACES env var (comma-separated, default empty = no auto-merge). In execute_remediation: if target namespace not in allowlist, serialize the patch as a GitOps PR (call GitHub API to open branch + PR with the manifest diff) and return {action: 'pr_created', pr_url: ...} instead of applying directly. If in allowlist, apply via kubectl/k8s client and return {action: 'applied'}. Allowlist membership check must be exact-match, not substring.
test_plan: Unit test: call execute_remediation with namespace='kube-system' (not in allowlist) and assert action=='pr_created' and k8s apply was NOT called. Unit test: call with namespace in allowlist and assert apply was called and no PR was created.

evidence: design-task — src/diagnostics_manager.py:111-125 troubleshoot_pod_issues hardcodes status='CrashLoopBackOff' and restart_count=5 for every pod regardless of input; no live detection of ImagePullBackOff, OOMKilled, or probe failures
diagnosis: The PRD must design a PodAnalyzer that detects real pod failure modes by inspecting live container statuses and events.
proposed_fix: PodAnalyzer.analyze(pod_name, namespace) -> PodDiagnosis: (1) core_v1.read_namespaced_pod to get live spec+status. (2) For each container_status: if waiting.reason in ['ImagePullBackOff','ErrImagePull'] emit FINDING_IMAGE_PULL with image name. (3) If waiting.reason=='CrashLoopBackOff' or restart_count>threshold emit FINDING_CRASH_LOOP; fetch last 100 log lines for root-cause snippet. (4) If last_state.terminated.reason=='OOMKilled' emit FINDING_OOM with memory limit. (5) Query events for FailedLiveness/FailedReadiness to emit FINDING_PROBE_FAIL. Return structured PodDiagnosis with per-container findings.
test_plan: Unit test: mock pod object with waiting.reason='ImagePullBackOff'; assert exactly one FINDING_IMAGE_PULL finding. Unit test: mock terminated reason='OOMKilled'; assert FINDING_OOM. Integration test: deploy a pod with an invalid image and call analyze; assert FINDING_IMAGE_PULL within 30 seconds.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:604-627 defines helm_status Tool schema; grep of entire src/ directory finds zero import of enhanced_tools in mcp_server.py (confirmed: grep returned no output); src/mcp_server.py:73-74 initializes self.helm_manager but handle_call_tool contains no elif for 'helm_status'
diagnosis: The helm_status schema is defined in enhanced_tools.py but enhanced_tools is never imported and helm_status is never registered as a callable tool in mcp_server.py, making it completely dead.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py handle_list_tools, add: `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_helm_tools` and include `*get_enhanced_helm_tools()` in the tools list. In handle_call_tool add: `elif name == 'helm_status': result = await self.helm_manager.helm_status(release_name=arguments['release_name'], namespace=arguments.get('namespace'), output_format=arguments.get('output_format','table'))`. Repeat the same pattern for all other enhanced tool names in the module.
test_plan: After wiring, call the MCP server with tool name 'helm_status' and a valid release name; assert the response contains stdout from `helm status`. Verify the tool appears in list_tools response.

evidence: GETTING_STARTED.md:141 — '45,720+ indexed Kubernetes GitHub issues'; GETTING_STARTED.md:142 — 'Real-time cluster analysis (if kubectl configured)'; GETTING_STARTED.md:149-153 — 'server automatically updates its knowledge base every hour'; GETTING_STARTED.md:70,201 — `curl http://localhost:3001/health`; GETTING_STARTED.md:219 — `curl http://localhost:3001/stats | jq`
diagnosis: The document overclaims a specific issue count (45,720+), live cluster analysis, hourly self-updating knowledge base, and HTTP health+stats endpoints — none of which are implemented: the server uses stdio (not HTTP), diagnostics are stubs, and health/stats routes do not exist.
proposed_fix: Remove the '45,720+' count claim (replace with 'indexed issues from monitored repositories'). Change 'Real-time cluster analysis' to 'Cluster analysis via local kubeconfig (requires kubectl configured)'. Remove the automatic hourly update claim or qualify it as 'when GITHUB_TOKEN is set'. Replace all `curl http://localhost:3001/...` examples with instructions for testing via the MCP stdio protocol (e.g., using the mcp CLI or a compatible client).
test_plan: After editing, grep the doc for '/health', '/stats', '45,720', 'automatically updates', and 'real-time' — all should be absent or replaced with accurate qualified statements.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:1-859 — file defines get_enhanced_kubectl_tools() and get_enhanced_helm_tools() returning lists of Tool schemas; confirmed by grep: zero import of this module exists in any other src/ file; src/mcp_server.py:73-74 initializes KubectlManager and HelmManager but none of the tool names defined in enhanced_tools.py appear in handle_call_tool dispatch
diagnosis: The entire enhanced_tools.py module is dead code: its 30+ tool schemas are never registered with the MCP server and never reachable by any client call.
proposed_fix: Import the module in mcp_server.py: `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools, get_enhanced_helm_tools`. In handle_list_tools extend the tools list: `*get_enhanced_kubectl_tools(), *get_enhanced_helm_tools()`. In handle_call_tool add dispatch branches mapping each tool name to the corresponding self.kubectl_manager or self.helm_manager method. A dispatch table (dict mapping name -> callable) is preferable to 30 elif branches.
test_plan: After wiring, call list_tools and assert the response includes 'kubectl_get', 'helm_status', and at least 28 other enhanced tool names. Call 'ping' tool and assert a successful response from KubectlManager.ping().

evidence: design-task — no watch/stream implementation exists in any src/ file; kubernetes.watch is not imported anywhere in the repo
diagnosis: The PRD must design an EventStreamWatcher that consumes the live Kubernetes event stream, classifies events, and routes actionable ones to the remediation queue.
proposed_fix: EventStreamWatcher(core_v1, remediation_queue: asyncio.Queue): async def watch(): use kubernetes.watch.Watch().stream(core_v1.list_event_for_all_namespaces, timeout_seconds=0) in a task. For each event: extract (reason, involved_object.kind, involved_object.name, namespace, message). Classify via a rule table: reason=='BackOff' and kind=='Pod' -> CRASH_LOOP; reason in ['FailedMount','FailedAttach'] -> PVC_MOUNT; reason=='OOMKilling' -> OOM. Enqueue a RemediationTask(kind, resource_ref, severity, raw_event) to remediation_queue. Reconnect with exponential backoff on connection errors. Stop via an asyncio.Event cancellation token.
test_plan: Unit test: feed a synthetic BackOff event into the classifier; assert CRASH_LOOP task is enqueued. Unit test: feed a FailedMount event; assert PVC_MOUNT task. Integration test: create a pod that crashes in a test namespace; assert EventStreamWatcher enqueues a CRASH_LOOP task within 10 seconds.

evidence: src/monitoring_manager.py:162-190 — entire performance_analysis method returns hardcoded dicts; src/monitoring_manager.py:55 — timestamp hardcoded to '2025-08-09T10:30:00Z'
diagnosis: performance_analysis is registered and dispatched in mcp_server.py:540-544 but the underlying MonitoringManager.performance_analysis and all five _analyze_* helpers return static fabricated metrics with no kubernetes client call anywhere in the file.
proposed_fix: Inject kubernetes.client (CoreV1Api, MetricsV1beta1Api) into MonitoringManager.__init__. In performance_analysis, call metrics_api.list_node_metrics() or metrics_api.list_namespaced_pod_metrics(namespace) based on analysis_type. Replace _analyze_cluster_performance and siblings with real API calls; raise a ValueError or return structured error if metrics-server is unavailable.
test_plan: Unit-test with a mocked kubernetes client that returns known MetricNodeList fixtures; assert the output reflects the fixture values. Integration test: call the tool against a real cluster and verify avg_response_time field matches prometheus or metrics-server data.

evidence: GettingStarted.md:18 — 'curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer/main/start.sh | bash'; GettingStarted.md:31-35 — 'curl http://localhost:3001/health'; GettingStarted.md:68 — 'Enjoy your production-ready Kubernetes platform engineering assistant!'

test_plan: Smoke-check: run the server and confirm curl http://localhost:3001/health returns a connection error; update doc accordingly.

evidence: coming_soon.md:179 — 'Code coverage maintained above 95%'; coming_soon.md:200 — 'Bug resolution time under 24 hours for critical issues'; coming_soon.md:196 — 'API response time under 2 seconds for 99% of requests'
diagnosis: The document presents aspirational targets (95% coverage, 24-hour SLA, 2s p99) as existing metrics/success criteria rather than goals, and lists dozens of unimplemented features with no indication that the current codebase is entirely stubbed out.
proposed_fix: Rename section 'Metrics & Success Criteria' to 'Target Metrics (not yet measured)'. Add a top-of-file disclaimer: 'All features listed here are planned; current implementation is stub-only.' Remove or qualify the SLA claims that imply an operational system.
test_plan: Review doc after edit to confirm no sentence presents a stub behavior as implemented; cross-check three randomly chosen feature claims against the source tree.

evidence: No guard exists in any manager file; src/mcp_server.py:309-328 — execute_remediation accepts any issue_type/target with dry_run defaulting to True but no namespace denylist check.
diagnosis: No enforcement layer exists to block auto-remediation against the namespaces ibkr-live-trader, daxxon-trading, and brightflow-live; any caller can pass those names to execute_remediation without restriction.
proposed_fix: Add TRADING_BLOCKED_NAMESPACES = {'ibkr-live-trader', 'daxxon-trading', 'brightflow-live'} constant. In KubernetesManager.execute_remediation (and KubectlManager destructive methods), extract namespace from target string; if it matches the set, raise PermissionError('Auto-remediation blocked for trading namespace; use PR workflow'). Log the attempt. Unit test: call execute_remediation with target='ibkr-live-trader/some-pod' and assert PermissionError is raised.
test_plan: Parameterized unit tests for all three blocked namespaces across execute_remediation, kubectl_delete, kubectl_scale; assert each raises PermissionError. Assert a non-trading namespace passes through.

evidence: k8s/deployment.yaml:37 — 'image: ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest' — pinned to 'latest', illustrating the class of issue to be remediated; no remediation logic exists in any manager.
diagnosis: No automated detection or fix exists for yanked/mutable image tags (e.g., bitnami/kubectl:1.30 removal); the design task is to implement a deterministic remediator that detects ImagePullBackOff caused by missing tags and proposes a concrete replacement.
proposed_fix: Data model: ImageTagIssue(namespace, pod, container, current_image, reason). Detection: poll pods in ImagePullBackOff; parse event message for 'not found' or 'manifest unknown'. Remediation: query registry API or known-good tag table for nearest semver; emit a patch command. Interface: async def remediate_image_tag(pod: str, namespace: str, dry_run: bool) -> RemediationResult. Edge cases: private registry auth, multi-container pods, digest-pinned images. Test plan: mock a pod with ImagePullBackOff and a registry stub returning 404; verify the remediator emits the correct patch with the fallback tag.
test_plan: Unit test with mocked CoreV1Api returning a pod with ImagePullBackOff condition; verify remediate_image_tag returns a non-empty patch command referencing a valid replacement tag.

evidence: k8s/kustomization.yaml:17-18 — 'name: k8s-platform-engineer-mcp / newTag: latest'; k8s/kustomization.yaml:27-30 — configMapGenerator uses 'config.env' file that does not exist in the repo; k8s/kustomization.yaml:32-35 — secretGenerator uses 'secrets.env' file that does not exist in the repo
diagnosis: The image tag is pinned to 'latest' (mutable, non-reproducible), and both configMapGenerator and secretGenerator reference .env files absent from the repo, causing kustomize build to fail on a fresh checkout.
proposed_fix: Pin newTag to a specific semver digest (e.g., 'v1.0.0' or a sha256). Add config.env.template and secrets.env.template to the repo with placeholder values, and document that ops must copy them before deployment. Alternatively, replace the generators with explicit ConfigMap/Secret manifests that are safe to commit.
test_plan: Run 'kustomize build k8s/' on a clean clone (without .env files) and verify it no longer errors. Run again after adding .env files from templates and verify all resources render.

evidence: No Finding dataclass or schema exists in the src/ tree; grep of src/ for 'Finding\|finding_id\|dpo_pair' returns no matches.
diagnosis: There is no structured Finding type for surfacing issues, generating PR descriptions, or producing DPO training pairs; the design task is to create a canonical serialization contract used by the remediator and audit agents.
proposed_fix: Define dataclass Finding with fields: id (uuid), component (str), kind (Literal['bug','security','stub','overclaim']), severity (Literal['critical','high','medium','low','info']), evidence (str), diagnosis (str), fix_class (str), proposed_fix (str), status (Literal['open','in_pr','resolved']). Add to_dict/from_dict. DPO pair: {'prompt': diagnosis+evidence, 'chosen': proposed_fix, 'rejected': ''}. Store in findings.jsonl, one object per line. Interface: FindingStore.add(finding), FindingStore.list(filter_fn), FindingStore.export_dpo().
test_plan: Unit test: create a Finding, serialize to dict, deserialize back, assert round-trip equality. Test export_dpo produces valid JSONL with 'prompt' and 'chosen' keys for each finding.

evidence: k8s/ingress.yaml:11 — 'nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "false"'; k8s/ingress.yaml:12-13 — TLS block commented out; k8s/ingress.yaml:21 — 'host: mcp-server.local'; k8s/ingress.yaml:29-31 — backend routes to port 3001 but server is stdio-only with no HTTP listener
diagnosis: The ingress disables TLS redirect, has no TLS configuration, uses a placeholder hostname, and routes to a port that the stdio-only MCP server never binds — making the ingress non-functional and insecure by default.
proposed_fix: Either delete ingress.yaml entirely (since the server is stdio-only and not HTTP-accessible) or, if an HTTP health/metrics endpoint is added later: enable ssl-redirect: 'true', uncomment the TLS block with a real cert-manager issuer annotation, replace mcp-server.local with a required variable, and route only to the metrics port (8080), not the MCP port.
test_plan: Validate with 'kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f k8s/ingress.yaml' and confirm no warnings. If TLS is enabled, verify cert-manager issues a certificate in a staging cluster.

evidence: push-and-deploy.sh:1 — only 'set -e', not 'set -euo pipefail'; push-and-deploy.sh:8 — hardcodes 'ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest' (mutable tag, no digest); push-and-deploy.sh:17 — 'kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml' applies only deployment, skipping namespace/rbac/configmap/secret ordering issues; push-and-deploy.sh — no check that docker login / GHCR token is set before push
diagnosis: The script lacks pipefail and nounset, pushes a mutable :latest tag without digest verification, applies only one manifest instead of the full kustomize stack, and silently succeeds if GHCR credentials are absent.
proposed_fix: Add 'set -euo pipefail' at line 1. Require VERSION env var and tag as ghcr.io/.../:${VERSION}. Replace single kubectl apply with 'kustomize build k8s/ | kubectl apply -f -'. Add a pre-flight check: 'docker manifest inspect ghcr.io/.../...latest || { echo "login required"; exit 1; }'.  Remove hardcoded org/image path; read from .env or env vars.
test_plan: Run with an unset GITHUB_TOKEN and verify the script exits non-zero. Run with VERSION unset and verify exit. Run with a valid VERSION against a test cluster and verify all kustomize resources are applied in correct order.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:106-128 — kubectl_delete Tool schema defined; grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' returns no matches (no import); grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'kubectl_delete' returns no matches (no dispatch case)
diagnosis: The kubectl_delete schema is defined in enhanced_tools.py and the handler exists in kubectl_manager.py:152, but enhanced_tools.py is never imported in mcp_server.py, so the tool is never registered and the handler is never reachable.

In handle_call_tool, add an elif branch: 'elif name == "kubectl_delete": result = await self.kubectl_manager.kubectl_delete(arguments["resource"], arguments["name"], arguments.get("namespace"))'. Apply the trading-ns hardblock guard before delegating.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:22-33 — self.client = None; self.api_client = None; initialize() contains only a comment 'Kubernetes client initialization would go here'

Raise NodeNotFoundError if node absent.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:166-210 — execute_remediation() builds a result dict with hardcoded string lists like 'Analyzed pod events and logs' and 'Would restart kubelet service'; no kubernetes client call is made anywhere in this method

evidence: k8s/configmap.yaml:1-19 — the file carries no immutable: true field; MCP_HOST is set to 0.0.0.0 with no comment restricting bind scope; no SECRET_GITHUB_TOKEN or KUBECONFIG keys are present yet deployment.yaml line 50 sets KUBECONFIG to a directory path, not a kubeconfig file
diagnosis: ConfigMap lacks immutability marking (allows silent hot-patching), exposes 0.0.0.0 bind without documentation of intent, and does not carry the KUBECONFIG value correction needed to match the broken env var in deployment.yaml.
proposed_fix: Add 'immutable: true' under metadata to prevent in-place mutations. Add inline comment that MCP_HOST=0.0.0.0 is intentional for pod networking. Remove MCP_HOST from ConfigMap and bind it in code defaults if this is not meant to be operator-configurable. Add GITHUB_ISSUES_UPDATE_INTERVAL comment noting unit is seconds.
test_plan: Run 'kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml' twice with a changed value and confirm the second apply is rejected due to immutability. Lint with kubeval or kube-score to verify no forbidden fields.

evidence: k8s/deployment.yaml:37 — image: ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest; k8s/kustomization.yaml:18 — newTag: latest; src/k8s_manager.py:54 — hardcoded 'version': 'v1.29.0'
diagnosis: No automated remediation behavior exists for yanked-image scenarios (e.g., bitnami/kubectl:1.30 class of issues); the codebase also uses :latest tags in both manifests and kustomization, which reproduces the exact vulnerability class this behavior is meant to fix.
proposed_fix: Implement ImageTagRemediator(target_deployment, target_namespace, new_image_ref): (1) validate new_image_ref matches format registry/repo:sha256@digest or semver tag, (2) call AppsV1Api().patch_namespaced_deployment with strategic merge patch setting spec.template.spec.containers[*].image, (3) watch rollout via AppsV1Api().read_namespaced_deployment_status until ready replicas == desired, (4) rollback via rollout undo if readiness timeout exceeded. Data model: ImageMigrationRecord(old_ref, new_ref, deployment, namespace, status, timestamp).
test_plan: Unit test: mock patch_namespaced_deployment and assert patch body contains correct image. Integration test: deploy a known :latest image, invoke remediator with pinned sha, confirm deployment.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image updated and pods reach Running.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:593-597 — dispatches to github_issues_manager.find_similar_issues(); src/github_issues_manager.py:478-516 — queries local SQLite DB at self.db_path; the DB is only populated if _fetch_recent_issues() succeeded at startup, which requires live GitHub API access
diagnosis: find_similar_issues() queries a local SQLite database that is empty on first run and will remain empty if GitHub API calls are rate-limited or the token is absent (self.github_token = None at line 56), so the tool silently returns [] with no indication that the database is unpopulated.
proposed_fix: Add a db_ready: bool flag set True only after _fetch_recent_issues completes at least one successful insert. In find_similar_issues(), check db_ready and return a structured error {'error': 'issue_db_not_ready', 'message': 'GitHub issue database is empty; set GITHUB_TOKEN env var and restart'} instead of []. Expose db record count in get_issue_statistics() response.
test_plan: Start manager without GITHUB_TOKEN and assert find_similar_issues returns error dict not empty list. Seed DB with 3 fixture issues; assert find_similar_issues returns matching entries for a query that matches title text.

evidence: src/ directory listing shows no cache.py or similar; grep across all src/*.py for 'cache' or 'TTL' yields zero hits in manager files
diagnosis: No LLM explanation cache exists anywhere in the codebase; repeated calls for the same finding hash will re-invoke the LLM backend on every request, causing redundant latency and cost.
proposed_fix: Implement FindingCache(ttl_seconds=3600): dict[str, CacheEntry] where key = sha256(json.dumps(finding, sort_keys=True)), CacheEntry = dataclass(explanation: str, created_at: float). get(key) checks time.monotonic() - entry.created_at < ttl_seconds; set(key, explanation) evicts entries older than TTL. Wire into LLM backend call site: check cache before invoking LLM, store result after. Cache is in-process; no external store required for v1.
test_plan: Unit test: insert entry, retrieve within TTL asserts hit, retrieve after TTL asserts miss. Integration test: call explanation endpoint twice with identical finding; assert LLM backend mock called exactly once.

evidence: src/ listing shows no llm_backend.py or nim.py; no import of openai, httpx, or NIM SDK appears in any src file; the mcp_server.py imports only internal managers
diagnosis: There is no pluggable LLM backend interface and no NIM integration; the server produces zero AI-generated explanations despite README claims of intelligent analysis, making the 'platform engineer' framing misleading.

Implement NIMBackend(LLMBackend) using openai.AsyncOpenAI(base_url=NIM_BASE_URL, api_key=NIM_API_KEY); call chat.completions.create(model='meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct', messages=[system_prompt, user_prompt_with_finding_json]). Inject backend into relevant managers via constructor. Default backend = NIMBackend if NIM_API_KEY env var is set, else raise ConfigurationError.
test_plan: Mock AsyncOpenAI.chat.completions.create; assert NIMBackend.explain passes finding JSON in user message and returns completion text. Test ConfigurationError raised when NIM_API_KEY absent.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:181-206 — execute_remediation returns ad-hoc dict with no schema; src/security_manager.py:48-56 — security_scan returns untyped dict; no dataclass, TypedDict, or Pydantic model named Finding, Issue, or similar exists anywhere in src/

evidence: k8s/kustomization.yaml:18 — newTag: latest; lines 29-35 — configMapGenerator and secretGenerator reference config.env and secrets.env which do not exist in k8s/ directory (confirmed by ls); line 24-25 — patchesStrategicMerge has a comment placeholder but no actual patches
diagnosis: kustomization.yaml references two non-existent env files (config.env, secrets.env) causing 'kustomize build' to fail hard; it also pins the image to :latest (no digest), breaking reproducible deployments and enabling supply-chain attacks.

evidence: No IngressAnalyzer class or module exists anywhere in src/; grep for 'IngressAnalyzer', 'ingress_analyzer', 'tls_expiry', 'host_conflict' returns nothing
diagnosis: IngressAnalyzer is entirely unimplemented — no backend-reachability check, no TLS expiry detection, and no host-conflict detection exist anywhere in the codebase.

evidence: No PVCAnalyzer, pvc_analyzer, or pvc_pending detection code found anywhere in src/; diagnostics_manager.py has no storage-specific check; monitoring_manager.py _analyze_storage_performance (line 303) returns hardcoded IOPS strings, not PVC status.

evidence: src/diagnostics_manager.py — troubleshoot_pod_issues method (called from mcp_server.py:501) exists but inspection (via monitoring_manager.py line 106 comment '# Stub implementation') confirms the entire diagnostics layer returns hardcoded data; no code in any src/ file calls kubernetes.client to inspect containerStatuses, lastState.terminated.reason, or probe failure events for real pods.

evidence: No IngressAnalyzer class exists anywhere in src/; grep returns zero hits for 'IngressAnalyzer', 'ingress.*analyz', 'tls.*expir'
diagnosis: IngressAnalyzer is entirely unimplemented; no backend-reachability check, TLS expiry detection, or host-conflict scan exists in the codebase.
proposed_fix: Create src/analyzers/ingress_analyzer.py extending BaseAnalyzer. Input: networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress list + Service list + TLS Secret list. Checks: (1) for each rule.backend.service, verify Service exists and selector matches running pod; (2) parse tls[].secretName, load Secret, decode cert, check NotAfter < now+30d; (3) detect duplicate host entries across Ingress objects. Output: list[Finding]. Dependencies: kubernetes.client.NetworkingV1Api, cryptography.x509.

evidence: No orphan job detection or cleanup logic exists in any src/*.py file; grep for 'orphan', 'ownerReference', 'job.*cleanup' returns zero results
diagnosis: There is no implementation that identifies Jobs lacking a valid ownerReference and queues them for deletion.
proposed_fix: In a new RemediationAction subclass OrphanJobCleaner: (1) list all Jobs in target namespace via BatchV1Api.list_namespaced_job; (2) filter jobs where metadata.ownerReferences is None or the referenced UID no longer exists in the CronJob list; (3) if dry_run=False, call BatchV1Api.delete_namespaced_job with propagation_policy='Background'; return list of deleted job names. Edge cases: running jobs (status.active > 0) must be excluded; respect a min-age threshold (e.g. 24h) to avoid racing a freshly-created job.
test_plan: Mock BatchV1Api responses with three jobs: one owned by active CronJob, one with stale ownerRef UID, one running; assert only the stale-ref job appears in cleanup output and the running job is skipped.

evidence: No Finding dataclass, TypedDict, or Pydantic model exists in any src/ file; grep for 'class Finding', 'Finding(', 'dataclass' returns zero results
diagnosis: There is no shared structured type for analyzer output, forcing each hypothetical analyzer to invent its own dict schema, making synthesis and PR/DPO generation ambiguous.
proposed_fix: Add src/models/finding.py: `@dataclass class Finding: id: str; component_id: str; kind: Literal['misconfiguration','degradation','security','orphan']; severity: Literal['critical','high','medium','low','info']; resource_ref: str; message: str; evidence: dict; remediation: Optional[str]; created_at: datetime`. Add `to_dict()` and `from_dict()` helpers. Import this type in all analyzers and the remediation queue.
test_plan: Round-trip test: construct a Finding, call to_dict(), call from_dict(result), assert all fields equal the original; test JSON serialization produces valid schema.

evidence: No CronJobAnalyzer class exists in any src/ file; grep for 'CronJobAnalyzer', 'missed.*schedule', 'lastScheduleTime' returns zero results
diagnosis: CronJob missed-schedule detection and orphaned-job attribution are entirely unimplemented.
proposed_fix: Create src/analyzers/cronjob_analyzer.py. Logic: (1) list CronJobs via BatchV1Api.list_namespaced_cron_job; (2) parse spec.schedule with croniter to compute expected next run; compare to status.lastScheduleTime — if delta > 2x period emit missed-schedule Finding; (3) list all Jobs; for each Job check ownerReferences[].name against CronJob names — jobs whose owner no longer exists are orphans; emit orphan Finding. Dependencies: croniter, kubernetes.client.BatchV1Api.
test_plan: Mock a CronJob with schedule '*/5 * * * *' and lastScheduleTime 30 minutes ago; assert one missed-schedule Finding is emitted. Mock a Job with ownerReference pointing to a non-existent CronJob; assert one orphan Finding is emitted.

evidence: No BaseAnalyzer abstract class exists in any src/ file; grep for 'BaseAnalyzer', 'ABC', 'abstractmethod' in src/ returns zero results
diagnosis: There is no abstract base class defining the analyzer contract, so all future analyzers have no enforced interface and no shared initialization logic.
proposed_fix: Add src/analyzers/base.py: `class BaseAnalyzer(ABC): def __init__(self, k8s_client): self.k8s = k8s_client; @abstractmethod async def analyze(self, namespace: Optional[str] = None) -> List[Finding]: ...; async def run_safe(self, namespace) -> List[Finding]: try: return await self.analyze(namespace); except Exception as e: return [Finding(..., message=str(e))]`. All analyzers inherit from this; the auto-remediator instantiates them polymorphically.

evidence: No event stream watcher exists in src/; grep for 'watch', 'Watch()', 'event_type', 'ADDED.*MODIFIED.*DELETED' in src/ returns zero results

evidence: k8s/pvc.yaml:1-16 — full file; no storageClassName validation (line 15 uses 'local-path' which is a cluster-specific provisioner not present by default); no annotations for backup or retention policy; no volumeMode field (defaults to Filesystem, undocumented); no ownerReference or labels linking PVC to a specific workload beyond the app label; file contains no resource limits beyond the storage request; no note about the PVC being unbound if local-path provisioner is absent

proposed_fix: Add a comment block explaining the local-path dependency and how to substitute (e.g. standard, gp2, hostpath). Add volumeMode: Filesystem explicitly. Add a note that admins should set storageClassName to match their cluster's default if local-path is unavailable.

evidence: k8s/pvc.yaml:15 — storageClassName: local-path

evidence: k8s/rbac.yaml:20 — resources: [..., "secrets", ...] verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
diagnosis: ClusterRole grants cluster-wide read access to all Secrets and to RBAC clusterroles/clusterrolebindings; this violates least-privilege and allows the server SA to enumerate credentials across every namespace.
proposed_fix: Split the ClusterRole into (1) a read-only ClusterRole that excludes 'secrets', and (2) a namespaced Role in 'mcp-kubernetes' only that grants 'secrets' get/list/watch. test_plan: Apply the revised manifests, then run 'kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:mcp-kubernetes:kubernetes-mcp-server-sa -n kube-system' and verify it returns 'no'.

evidence: mcp-port-forward.sh:1-7 — 'set -euo pipefail' is present but line 56: 'kubectl port-forward ... &' forks a background process whose PID is not captured; lines 99-107: test_connectivity calls curl twice (line 100 for exit code, line 101 for response body), doubling the network call.

evidence: k8s/deployment.yaml:37 — image: ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest; line 90: readOnlyRootFilesystem: false; line 49-51: KUBECONFIG env value is set to the service account directory path, not a valid kubeconfig file.
diagnosis: Three combined issues: (1) image is pinned to mutable 'latest' tag making deploys non-reproducible; (2) readOnlyRootFilesystem is false, widening the attack surface; (3) KUBECONFIG is set to the service account directory, not a real kubeconfig, so in-cluster auth will fail.
proposed_fix: Pin image to a specific digest or semver tag (e.g. ':1.0.0@sha256:...'). Set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and add an emptyDir volume for any writable paths needed at runtime. Remove the KUBECONFIG env var entirely (kubernetes python client auto-detects in-cluster config via KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST/PORT which are already set).
test_plan: Lint with 'kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f k8s/deployment.yaml' after changes. Run a Polaris or kube-score scan and assert no 'latest tag' or 'readOnlyRootFilesystem=false' findings. Deploy to a test namespace and verify pod reaches Running state with no kubeconfig errors in logs.

evidence: k8s/namespace.yaml:1-9 — only metadata labels are present; no ResourceQuota, LimitRange, or NetworkPolicy resources are included or referenced.

Add a default LimitRange so containers without explicit limits get sensible defaults. Add a default-deny NetworkPolicy allowing only ingress on port 3001 from the cluster's ingress namespace.
test_plan: Apply the updated namespace.yaml and verify 'kubectl describe quota -n mcp-kubernetes' shows the expected limits. Attempt to create a pod exceeding the quota and confirm it is rejected. Verify the NetworkPolicy blocks cross-namespace traffic with a curl test pod.

evidence: No existing implementation found anywhere in the repo (grep returns nothing for 'pull_request', 'pr_body', 'gitops').

test_plan: Mock PyGithub in unit tests asserting PR title format, body sections, and label values; integration test creates a real PR on a fork and verifies label and branch naming.

evidence: No DONE gate, checklist, or CI step found anywhere in the repo (grep returns nothing for 'done_definition', 'definition_of_done', 'failed_pods').
diagnosis: No machine-enforceable done definition exists; the project has no gate preventing a story from being marked done without passing evidence.
proposed_fix: Define DoneGate as a CI step that asserts: (1) pytest exits 0 with no skipped tests; (2) docker build exits 0 and image digest is recorded; (3) kubectl get events -n mcp-kubernetes --field-selector type=Warning returns zero lines; (4) kubectl get pods -n mcp-kubernetes --field-selector status.phase!=Running,status.phase!=Succeeded returns no rows. Implement as a shell function done_gate() in a ci/done_gate.sh that returns non-zero if any assertion fails. Wire into the GitHub Actions workflow as a final required job.
test_plan: Create a test that intentionally leaves a pod in CrashLoopBackOff and verify done_gate.sh exits non-zero; verify it exits 0 after resolving the pod.

evidence: deploy-k8s.sh:6 — 'set -e' (missing -u and -o pipefail); deploy-k8s.sh:62-63 — 'CLUSTER_SERVER=$(kubectl cluster-info | grep "control plane" | awk ...)' (pipeline failure ignored without pipefail); deploy-k8s.sh:75 — 'eval $(minikube docker-env)' (eval of unquoted command substitution); deploy-k8s.sh:146-158 — background port-forward PID not trapped on script exit (SIGINT/ERR leave orphan process)
diagnosis: The script uses only 'set -e', omitting '-u' and '-o pipefail', allowing silent failures in pipelines; eval of minikube output is an injection vector; the background port-forward process is not cleaned up on abnormal exit.
proposed_fix: Line 6: change to 'set -euo pipefail'. Line 75: quote minikube output or use 'minikube -p "$CLUSTER_NAME" docker-env --shell bash | source /dev/stdin'. Add a trap: 'trap "kill \$PORT_FORWARD_PID 2>/dev/null" EXIT INT TERM' immediately after line 146. Add 'shellcheck' run to CI for this file.
test_plan: Run shellcheck against the patched script with no warnings; write a bats test that kills the script midway and verifies no port-forward process remains in the process table.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:532-537 — 'result = await self.security_manager.security_scan(...)'; src/security_manager.py:92-116 — '_scan_rbac returns hardcoded dict: total_roles: 15, issues: [{severity: high, resource: default:admin-sa}]'; src/security_manager.py:199 — 'encryption_at_rest: True' hardcoded unconditionally
diagnosis: security_scan returns entirely fabricated data including fake CVEs, fake RBAC issues, and a hardcoded 'encryption_at_rest: True' flag — a user relying on this for compliance audits receives false assurance.
proposed_fix: In _scan_rbac: call RbacAuthorizationV1Api().list_cluster_role_binding() and inspect subjects/roleRef for cluster-admin bindings. In _scan_secrets: call CoreV1Api().list_namespaced_secret() and inspect for plaintext-looking data values. Remove the hardcoded 'encryption_at_rest: True'; derive it from the kube-apiserver --encryption-provider-config flag if accessible, otherwise report 'unknown'. All hardcoded issue lists must be deleted.
test_plan: Unit test with a mock RbacAuthorizationV1Api returning a known ClusterRoleBinding to cluster-admin; assert the scan output contains exactly that binding as a high-severity issue. Verify that 'encryption_at_rest' is never hardcoded True in the response.

evidence: No caching layer found anywhere in src/ (grep for 'cache', 'ttl', 'lru' returns nothing relevant in the manager files).
diagnosis: No LLM explanation cache exists; repeated calls for the same finding will re-invoke the LLM every time, causing unnecessary latency and cost.
proposed_fix: Data model: cache key = sha256(finding_id + evidence_text), value = {explanation: str, created_at: datetime, ttl_seconds: int=3600}. Store in a dict[str, CacheEntry] on a new ExplanationCache class. Interface: async def get_or_generate(finding: Finding, generator: Callable) -> str. On miss, call generator, store result with timestamp. On hit, check age against TTL and return cached value. Evict entries older than TTL on each read. Use asyncio.Lock per key to prevent cache stampede.
test_plan: Unit test: call get_or_generate twice with the same finding; verify generator is called exactly once. Test TTL expiry by monkeypatching datetime. Test concurrent calls with asyncio.gather and verify generator is not called more than once per key.

evidence: src/config.py:47 — 'admission_controllers: List[str] = None' (mutable default using None sentinel, not field(default_factory=list)); src/config.py:59 — lists PodSecurityPolicy in default admission_controllers (PSP was removed in k8s 1.25+); src/config.py:143-149 — load_config() always returns a fresh ServerConfig() with no validation; no __post_init__ validation ensures kubeconfig_path actually exists or that port is in a valid range
diagnosis: SecurityConfig uses a None-sentinel workaround instead of dataclass field(default_factory), includes a removed k8s admission controller (PodSecurityPolicy), and load_config performs no input validation.
proposed_fix: Line 47: change to 'admission_controllers: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)' and add 'from dataclasses import field'. Line 59: remove 'PodSecurityPolicy' from default list; replace with 'NodeRestriction'. In ServerConfig.__post_init__: add range check 'if not (1 <= self.port <= 65535): raise ValueError(f"Invalid port: {self.port}")'. Add optional kubeconfig existence check with a logged warning.
test_plan: Unit tests: verify SecurityConfig() default list does not contain PodSecurityPolicy; verify ServerConfig with port=0 raises ValueError; verify load_config() with KUBECONFIG pointing to a nonexistent path logs a warning without raising.

evidence: k8s/configmap.yaml:1-19 — entire file shown; no sensitive values; k8s/deployment.yaml:37 — 'image: ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest' (latest tag, not pinned SHA or semver)
diagnosis: The ConfigMap itself contains no sensitive data and is structurally correct, but it references 'latest' image tag in the companion Deployment (deployment.yaml:37) which can cause unpredictable rollouts; the ConfigMap has no ownerReferences and will survive namespace deletion if namespace is force-deleted.
proposed_fix: In deployment.yaml line 37: pin the image to a specific semver tag or SHA digest, e.g. 'ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:1.0.0' or append '@sha256:<digest>'. Document in the README that 'latest' must never be used in production. The ConfigMap itself requires no structural changes beyond adding a 'app.kubernetes.io/version' label consistent with the Deployment.
test_plan: CI lint step: run 'grep -r ":latest"' across k8s/ and fail if any match is found; kustomize build and kubeval to confirm manifest schema validity.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:87-104 — Tool(name='kubectl_apply', ...) defined; grep across all src/ for 'enhanced_tools' or 'get_enhanced' returns only definitions within enhanced_tools.py itself — zero import lines in mcp_server.py or any other file
diagnosis: The kubectl_apply tool schema is defined in enhanced_tools.py but the module is never imported by mcp_server.py, so the tool is never registered with the MCP server and is completely unreachable by any client.
proposed_fix: In src/mcp_server.py, add: 'from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools, get_enhanced_helm_tools'. In handle_list_tools(), extend the returned tools list: 'tools=[...existing..., *get_enhanced_kubectl_tools(), *get_enhanced_helm_tools()]'. In handle_call_tool(), add elif branches for each enhanced tool name dispatching to self.kubectl_manager or self.helm_manager methods. Alternatively, if the decision is to delete: remove enhanced_tools.py entirely and remove KubectlManager/HelmManager instantiation from __init__ since they serve no purpose without routing.
test_plan: After wiring: call list_tools via the MCP protocol and assert 'kubectl_apply' appears in the response; call the tool with a minimal pod YAML and assert it reaches kubectl_manager.kubectl_apply without error. If deleted: assert the file no longer exists and no dead imports remain.

evidence: No vcluster integration exists anywhere in src/ or tests/ — grep finds zero references to vcluster, sandbox, or ephemeral cluster lifecycle
diagnosis: There is no ephemeral sandbox mechanism; any candidate fix the remediator produces is either applied directly to the real cluster or never tested at all.
proposed_fix: Data model: SandboxSession { id, vcluster_name, kubeconfig_path, created_at, status }. Interface: async create_sandbox() -> SandboxSession, async apply_fix(session, manifest_yaml), async smoke_test(session, probe_spec) -> bool, async destroy_sandbox(session). Dependencies: vcluster CLI or Helm chart, temp kubeconfig file, 10-second timeout probe. Edge cases: vcluster pod never becomes ready (timeout + cleanup), smoke test flakes (retry 3x), destroy must succeed even if create partially failed.
test_plan: Unit: mock vcluster CLI calls, assert kubeconfig written and cleaned up. Integration: spin a real vcluster in CI, apply a broken manifest, assert smoke_test returns False, assert cluster is destroyed. Measure e2e wall time <= 60s.

evidence: No ServiceAnalyzer class exists in src/; k8s_manager.py returns hardcoded cluster info (confirmed by prior audit finding). No endpoint or selector-mismatch detection logic is present.
diagnosis: ServiceAnalyzer is entirely unimplemented — no code checks for missing endpoints or selector mismatches between Service and Pod label sets.
proposed_fix: Interface: async analyze_service(namespace, service_name) -> ServiceAnalysis. Steps: (1) fetch Service via v1.read_namespaced_service; (2) fetch EndpointSlice or Endpoints for the service; (3) if endpoints.subsets is empty, flag 'no_endpoints'; (4) list Pods matching service.spec.selector; (5) if pod list empty but selector non-empty, flag 'selector_mismatch'; (6) diff selector keys against pod labels to produce 'missing_label' detail. Return structured ServiceAnalysis with severity and suggested fix. Edge cases: headless services, ExternalName type, selector-less Services.
test_plan: Unit tests: mock k8s client returning a service with selector {app: foo} but no matching pods — assert selector_mismatch detected. Mock service with 0 endpoint subsets — assert no_endpoints detected. Integration test against a real misconfigured service in a vcluster sandbox.

evidence: documentation_manager.py:275 — generate_test_documentation() in conftest.py injects docs WITHOUT a 'troubleshooting' key, but documentation_manager.py:406 does `for trouble in doc['troubleshooting']` with no key-existence guard, causing a KeyError at runtime whenever the search DB contains hand-loaded docs.
diagnosis: get_troubleshooting_guide and search_documentation iterate over doc['troubleshooting'] without a .get() fallback, making the manager crash on any doc entry that lacks that key.
proposed_fix:
Line 406: change `for trouble in doc['troubleshooting']:` to `for trouble in doc.get('troubleshooting', []):`
Line 347: same fix for `for command in doc['commands']:` -> `doc.get('commands', [])`
Line 344: `for section in doc['sections']:` -> `doc.get('sections', [])`
Also: _build_documentation_index() at line 64 uses asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) but never checks for exceptions in the returned list — add a check and log each failed task.
test_plan: Unit test: insert a doc entry without 'troubleshooting' key into documentation_db, call get_troubleshooting_guide('anything') — assert no exception and empty list returned. Existing test in conftest already provides such entries, so running the test suite will verify the fix.

evidence: security-scan-demo.sh:1 — shebang is `#!/bin/bash` with no `set -euo pipefail`; lines 14-18 pipe kubectl through jq without checking exit codes; line 64 uses `[ "$NETWORK_POLICIES" -lt 1 ]` but `wc -l | tr -d ' '` can return empty string on error, causing arithmetic errors.
diagnosis: Missing `set -euo pipefail` means any kubectl failure is silently swallowed and the script reports false-positive clean results; the empty-string arithmetic comparison on line 64 will also crash the script unpredictably.
proposed_fix:
Add `set -euo pipefail` after shebang (line 2).
Wrap each kubectl command in a subshell with error capture: `ROOT_PODS=$(kubectl get pods -A -o json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '...' || echo '')`
Change arithmetic guards to: `[[ "${NETWORK_POLICIES:-0}" -lt 1 ]]`
Replace emoji in echo statements with plain text for non-UTF-8 terminals.
Add a `command -v kubectl` and `command -v jq` preflight check at top.
test_plan: Run shellcheck against the fixed script — expect zero warnings. Run in a context where kubectl is unavailable and verify the script exits non-zero with a clear error message rather than reporting false-positives.

evidence: k8s/pvc.yaml:1-16 — the manifest contains no securityContext, no VolumeMode, no selector, and hardcodes `storageClassName: local-path` without documentation. No annotations for backup policy or retention. The namespace `mcp-kubernetes` is assumed to exist but no Namespace manifest is present in k8s/.
diagnosis: The PVC is under-specified: it lacks volume mode declaration, storage class is environment-specific with no override mechanism, and there is no indication of encryption-at-rest requirements or backup annotations.
proposed_fix:
Add `volumeMode: Filesystem` to spec.
Add annotation `backup.velero.io/backup-volumes: 'kubernetes-mcp-server-data'` in metadata.
Add a comment block explaining why `local-path` is chosen and that operators should override via Kustomize for production.
Add a companion `namespace.yaml` creating `mcp-kubernetes` with `Pod Security Admission` labels (baseline or restricted).
Parameterize `storageClassName` via a Kustomize patch target.
test_plan: Run `kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f k8s/pvc.yaml` against a cluster with local-path-provisioner installed and verify no errors. Run `kubeconform` or `kube-score` against the manifest and assert score >= 80.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py — grep for 'enhanced_tools' returns no output; grep for 'helm_uninstall' and 'uninstall_helm_chart' returns no output. The schema `uninstall_helm_chart` is defined in enhanced_tools.py:503-525 but mcp_server.py never imports enhanced_tools.
diagnosis: enhanced_tools.py is entirely dead code — mcp_server.py does not import it, so none of its 30+ tool schemas (including helm_uninstall) are registered or callable by any MCP client.
proposed_fix:
Option A (wire-up): In mcp_server.py, import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools, get_enhanced_helm_tools from enhanced_tools and extend the tools list returned by handle_list_tools(). Add dispatch branches in handle_call_tool() mapping each enhanced tool name to the corresponding kubectl_manager or helm_manager method.
Option B (delete): If enhanced_tools.py is intended to be replaced entirely by the inline tools in mcp_server.py, delete enhanced_tools.py and document the deduplication decision.
Choice depends on whether enhanced_tools.py contains schemas that are NOT already in mcp_server.py inline list (they do overlap significantly — audit required before wiring).
test_plan: After wiring: call `list_tools` via the MCP protocol and assert `uninstall_helm_chart` appears. Call `uninstall_helm_chart` with a mocked helm_manager and assert the handler dispatches to `helm_manager.uninstall_chart`. After deleting: assert no import of enhanced_tools anywhere and all previously-tested tools still appear.

evidence: No audit log module, table, or write path exists anywhere in src/ — grep for 'audit_log', 'audit log', 'append_audit', 'audit_entry' returns zero matches.
diagnosis: Every remediator action is unaudited; there is no who/what/when/evidence trail, making post-incident review and compliance impossible.
proposed_fix: Data model: AuditEntry { id UUID, timestamp ISO8601, actor str ('remediator'|user_id), action str, target_resource str, namespace str, evidence dict, outcome str ('success'|'failure'|'skipped'), duration_ms int }. Interface: async append(entry: AuditEntry). Storage: append-only SQLite table `audit_log` (WAL mode) or NDJSON file with rotation. Integration points: wrap every manager method call in mcp_server.handle_call_tool() to write an entry before and after execution. Expose read-only `get_audit_log(since, limit)` MCP tool. Edge cases: write failure must not block the action; use fire-and-forget with error log.
test_plan: Unit: call any tool handler and assert an AuditEntry was written with correct action and timestamp. Test write failure path — mock storage to raise IOError and assert tool still returns a result. Integration: run 5 tool calls, query get_audit_log, assert 5 entries with non-null timestamps and matching action names.

evidence: No state machine, iteration loop, or PRD pipeline exists in src/ — grep for 'state_machine', 'iteration', 'review_loop', 'implement_loop' returns zero matches.
diagnosis: The repo has no orchestration layer connecting review -> PRD -> split -> implement -> verify; every step is manual, making automated remediation impossible.
proposed_fix: States: IDLE -> REVIEWING -> PRD_GENERATED -> SPLIT -> IMPLEMENTING -> VERIFYING -> DONE | FAILED. Transitions: REVIEWING on new issue detected; PRD_GENERATED after analyzer produces a PRD; SPLIT after PRD decomposed into atomic tasks; IMPLEMENTING per-task (re-entrant); VERIFYING after all tasks complete; DONE if verify passes; FAILED if max_iterations (default 3) exceeded or smoke test fails. Interface: IterationStateMachine.run(issue_context) -> RemediationResult. Stop conditions: (1) smoke test passes; (2) max_iterations exceeded; (3) unrecoverable error (e.g., vcluster create fails). Persist state to SQLite so crashes are recoverable.
test_plan: Unit: mock each stage and assert correct state transitions for happy path and for max_iterations exceeded. Integration: inject a synthetic misconfigured Deployment, run the state machine, assert it reaches DONE within 3 iterations with vcluster sandbox smoke test passing.
