evidence: No NetPolAnalyzer or network policy analysis logic exists in src/ — grep for 'networkpolicy', 'network_policy', 'NetPol', 'orphan_policy' returns zero matches in src/.
diagnosis: Network policy analysis is entirely unimplemented; orphan policies and denied flows cannot be detected.
proposed_fix: Interface: async analyze_network_policies(namespace) -> NetPolAnalysis. Steps: (1) list all NetworkPolicies in namespace; (2) for each policy, list pods matching spec.podSelector — if empty, flag 'orphan_policy'; (3) build an allowed-flow matrix from ingress/egress rules; (4) list all Services and their backing pods; (5) for each service, check if a NetworkPolicy denies ingress from expected sources — flag 'denied_flow' with source/destination/port detail. Return NetPolAnalysis { orphan_policies: [], denied_flows: [], unreachable_services: [] }. Edge cases: default-deny namespace policies, CIDR-based rules, empty podSelector (matches all pods).
test_plan: Unit: mock k8s client with a NetworkPolicy whose podSelector matches no pods — assert orphan_policy flagged. Mock a policy that blocks port 80 from a frontend service to a backend — assert denied_flow detected. Integration: apply known-bad network policies in a vcluster sandbox and run analyzer.

evidence: test_cluster_management.py:108 — `k8s_manager.resolve_node_not_ready = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resolution)` then line 111 calls the same mock and asserts against the injected dict. Line 213 uses fixture `cluster_manager` which is NOT defined in tests/conftest.py (grep finds zero definition), meaning tests 3-4 (etcd, apiserver) will fail at collection with fixture error.
diagnosis: Every test in this file is a tautology — each test sets a mock return value then asserts that same value was returned, exercising only Python's Mock library, not any production code. Additionally, `cluster_manager` fixture is undefined, causing collection failures for at least 2 tests.
proposed_fix:
1. Delete all AsyncMock assignment patterns and instead call real manager methods with a mocked kubernetes client (as conftest.py already wires mock v1/apps_v1 clients).
2. Add `cluster_manager` fixture to conftest.py as an alias or wrapper around `k8s_manager` for control-plane-scoped tests.
3. Each test should assert on the OUTPUT of real logic, not on data the test itself injected.
4. Minimum coverage gates: assert the k8s API was actually called (e.g., `manager.v1.list_node.assert_called_once()`).
test_plan: Run pytest with --tb=short and verify zero fixture-not-found errors. Measure real branch coverage with pytest-cov — target >=60% on k8s_manager.py for these scenarios. Ensure no test passes solely because it asserts on its own mock input.

evidence: src/monitoring_manager.py:220-230 — _analyze_node_resources returns hardcoded strings: 'cpu_usage': '42%', 'memory_usage': '58%', 'disk_usage': '65%', 'status': 'healthy' with no k8s client call
diagnosis: NodeAnalyzer behavior does not exist; the closest method _analyze_node_resources returns static fixture data and never reads node conditions (NotReady, DiskPressure, MemoryPressure, PIDPressure) from the kubernetes client.
proposed_fix: Add NodeAnalyzer class with method analyze_node(node_name) -> NodeAnalysis. Inject CoreV1Api; call core_v1.read_node(node_name). Parse .status.conditions for type in [Ready, DiskPressure, MemoryPressure, PIDPressure]; map status=True/Unknown to severity enum. Return structured NodeAnalysis(node_name, conditions: list[NodeCondition], taint_list, allocatable_vs_capacity). Raise NodeNotFoundError for unknown nodes.
test_plan: Mock CoreV1Api.read_node to return a V1Node with each pressure condition set to True in turn; assert analyzer returns the matching severity. Integration test against a real kubeconfig context with a node in DiskPressure.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:604-627 — helm_status Tool schema defined. grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' returns zero matches; the file is never imported.
diagnosis: The helm_status schema is declared in enhanced_tools.py but mcp_server.py does not import enhanced_tools at all, so the tool is never registered and is dead code.
proposed_fix: In src/mcp_server.py add: from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_helm_tools. In _register_handlers, extend the tools list with get_enhanced_helm_tools(). Add a dispatch case for 'helm_status' that calls self.helm_manager.get_status(release_name, namespace, output_format) and returns TextContent. Implement helm_manager.get_status() using subprocess(['helm','status',release_name,'-n',namespace,'-o',output_format]).
test_plan: Unit test: mock helm_manager.get_status; invoke the MCP tool handler with name='helm_status'; assert dispatch calls get_status with correct args. Integration test: run helm_status against a real release and verify JSON output contains 'status' key.

evidence: k8s/deployment.yaml:37 — image: ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest; line 90 — readOnlyRootFilesystem: false; line 50 — KUBECONFIG value: '/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount' (a directory, not a file)
diagnosis: Three security/correctness issues: (1) image pinned to :latest prevents reproducible deploys; (2) readOnlyRootFilesystem: false leaves the container filesystem writable; (3) KUBECONFIG env var points to the serviceaccount directory rather than the token file, so in-cluster auth will fail if the code tries to use KUBECONFIG.
proposed_fix: Line 37: pin image to a SHA digest or semver tag (e.g. :1.0.0@sha256:...). Line 90: set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and add emptyDir volume mounts for /tmp and any writable paths the app needs. Lines 49-50: remove the KUBECONFIG env var entirely; the python kubernetes client auto-detects in-cluster credentials from the serviceaccount projection without KUBECONFIG.
test_plan: Run kubectl apply --dry-run=server against a test cluster; verify pod starts and /health probe returns 200. Run kubesec scan on the manifest and confirm score improves. Confirm python kubernetes client can list pods using in-cluster auth in a CI job.

evidence: tests/production/test_network_service_issues.py:82-85 — k8s_manager.resolve_service_no_endpoints = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resolution); resolution = await k8s_manager.resolve_service_no_endpoints(service_context). Identical pattern on lines 157-160, 233-235, 308-311, 384-387, 459-462, 540-543, 625-629, 700-703. Fixtures network_manager, service_mesh_manager, storage_manager are never defined in conftest.py (grep returns zero definitions).
diagnosis: Every test patches the method under test with AsyncMock returning the expected dict, then asserts against that same dict — no production code path is exercised; additionally three fixture names (network_manager, service_mesh_manager, storage_manager) are undefined and will cause collection errors.
proposed_fix: Remove AsyncMock self-return pattern; instead call the real manager method and mock only the kubernetes client layer (core_v1, apps_v1). Add conftest fixtures: network_manager returns a real NetworkManager (if it exists) or KubernetesManager with mocked client; likewise for service_mesh_manager and storage_manager. Tests should assert on behavior, not on the mock's own return value.
test_plan: Run pytest tests/production/test_network_service_issues.py --collect-only and verify zero collection errors. Run with coverage; confirm each test exercises at least one non-trivial code branch in the manager under test.

evidence: No OrphanJobCleanup class or method exists anywhere in src/. grep -rn 'orphan\|job.*cleanup\|cleanup.*job' src/ returns no matches.
diagnosis: The orphan-job-cleanup behavior is entirely unimplemented; there is no data model, controller watch loop, or ownership-check logic anywhere in the codebase.
proposed_fix: Data model: OrphanJob(name, namespace, uid, created_at, owner_references: list). Interface: async def find_orphan_jobs(namespace='') -> list[OrphanJob] — calls batch_v1.list_namespaced_job; filters jobs where ownerReferences is empty or references a deleted object (check core_v1.read_namespaced_pod_template raises 404). async def cleanup_orphan_jobs(jobs: list[OrphanJob], dry_run=True) -> CleanupResult. Edge cases: jobs with active pods must not be deleted; respect non-destructive_mode flag; emit audit log entry per deletion.
test_plan: Unit: mock batch_v1.list_namespaced_job with a mix of owned and unowned jobs; assert find_orphan_jobs returns only unowned ones. Unit: assert cleanup_orphan_jobs with dry_run=True makes no delete calls. Integration: create a real orphan job in a test namespace and verify cleanup removes it.

evidence: k8s/configmap.yaml:15 — RBAC_ENABLED: 'true'; line 16 — POD_SECURITY_STANDARDS: 'true'; line 17 — NETWORK_POLICIES: 'true'. These flags are declared but no corresponding code in src/ reads or enforces them (grep -rn 'RBAC_ENABLED\|POD_SECURITY_STANDARDS\|NETWORK_POLICIES' src/ returns zero matches).
diagnosis: The ConfigMap advertises security feature flags (RBAC_ENABLED, POD_SECURITY_STANDARDS, NETWORK_POLICIES) that are never consumed by application code, creating a false impression of enforced security controls.
proposed_fix: In config.py, add fields rbac_enabled, pod_security_standards, network_policies sourced from env vars. In security_manager.py, gate RBAC check logic on self.config.rbac_enabled. Remove or rename flags that have no enforcement path to avoid misleading operators. If enforcement is out of scope, replace with a comment documenting they are informational only.
test_plan: Unit test: set RBAC_ENABLED=false in env; assert security_manager skips RBAC checks. Set RBAC_ENABLED=true; assert checks run. Confirm no test previously relying on the flag being a no-op breaks.

evidence: tests/production/test_pod_issues.py:70-74 — mcp_server.resolve_issue_end_to_end = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_pipeline_result); result = await mcp_server.resolve_issue_end_to_end(issue_context). KubernetesPlatformEngineerMCPServer has no resolve_issue_end_to_end method (grep returns zero matches in src/). Same self-mock pattern on lines 150-157, 220-223, 287-291, 355-358, 424-427, 503-505, 583-586, 673-675, 766-769.
diagnosis: Every test in this file mocks a method that does not exist on the target class and asserts against its own fabricated return value, providing zero coverage of real production code while giving the appearance of a complete test suite.
proposed_fix: Replace the self-mock pattern with tests that call real methods (analyze_pod, diagnose_crashloop, etc.) on managers with only the kubernetes client mocked. For test_crashloopbackoff_complete_resolution, call diagnostics_manager.analyze_pod_issue('CrashLoopBackOff', pod_context) with mock apps_v1 returning a pod with restart_count=5; assert the returned dict contains 'solution_steps' and 'kubectl_commands' derived from actual logic.
test_plan: Run pytest --collect-only and verify no AttributeError on undefined methods. Run with --cov=src and confirm branch coverage for DiagnosticsManager and KubernetesManager rises above 40% (currently near 0% from real code paths).

evidence: src/monitoring_manager.py:55-56 — hardcoded: 'timestamp': '2025-08-09T10:30:00Z'. Lines 99-100 — 'total_entries': 1247, 'filtered_entries': 156. Lines 107-113 — log_summary with static error_count=23, warning_count=67. Lines 197-217 — _analyze_cluster_resources returns static dict with 'total_cores': 12, 'used_cores': 5.4. Lines 220-230, 232-243, 244-254 — all private _analyze_* methods return fully static dicts with no kubernetes client calls.
diagnosis: MonitoringManager contains zero real kubernetes client calls; all analysis methods return hardcoded fixture data, including a frozen timestamp from 2025-08-09, making every metric and log response fabricated.
proposed_fix: Inject CoreV1Api and CustomObjectsApi (for metrics-server). In _analyze_cluster_resources, call custom_objects_api.list_cluster_custom_object('metrics.k8s.io','v1beta1','nodes') to get real CPU/memory. In analyze_logs, call core_v1.read_namespaced_pod_log with timestamps=True and since_seconds derived from time_range. Replace hardcoded timestamp with datetime.utcnow().isoformat()+'Z'. Add a feature flag to fall back to stub data when metrics-server is unavailable.
test_plan: Mock CustomObjectsApi to return a realistic NodeMetrics response; assert _analyze_cluster_resources returns cpu.utilization_percent matching the mock data. Assert timestamp in result is within 5 seconds of test execution time.

evidence: No ServiceAnalyzer class or analyze_service method exists in src/. grep -rn 'ServiceAnalyzer\|selector.*mismatch\|no.*endpoint' src/ returns zero matches. k8s_manager.py has no endpoint-inspection logic.
diagnosis: The ServiceAnalyzer behavior (detecting no-endpoints and selector mismatch conditions) is entirely absent from the codebase; there is no data model, no kubernetes client call, and no diagnostic output for service connectivity failures.
proposed_fix: Data model: ServiceAnalysis(service_name, namespace, selector: dict, endpoint_count: int, matching_pod_count: int, issues: list[str]). Interface: async def analyze_service(name, namespace) -> ServiceAnalysis. Implementation: call core_v1.read_namespaced_service to get .spec.selector; call core_v1.read_namespaced_endpoints to get subset addresses; call core_v1.list_namespaced_pod with label_selector built from selector to count matching pods. Compare endpoint_count vs matching_pod_count to detect selector mismatch vs pod-not-ready issues. Edge cases: ExternalName services have no selector; headless services have clusterIP=None.
test_plan: Unit: mock read_namespaced_service with selector app=foo; mock list_namespaced_pod returning 2 pods with label app=foo; mock read_namespaced_endpoints returning 0 addresses; assert issues contains 'pods not ready'. Unit: mock endpoints returning 2 addresses; assert issues is empty.

evidence: K8S_ANNOUNCEMENT.md:11 — 'Monitors 17+ major Kubernetes ecosystem repos... Automatically updates its knowledge base every hour'. K8S_ANNOUNCEMENT.md:18 — 'Production-Ready Kubernetes Deployment'. K8S_ANNOUNCEMENT.md:27 — 'Automated Remediation & Diagnostics: Deep cluster health checks, pod/network diagnostics, resource analysis'. src/monitoring_manager.py returns static hardcoded data; src/github_issues_manager.py (not assigned but confirmed by other agents) uses SQLite with no live GitHub polling confirmed. src/mcp_server.py has no import of enhanced_tools; 18+ tool schemas are dead.
diagnosis: The announcement document makes at least three falsifiable production-grade claims — hourly repo monitoring, automated remediation, and deep real-time diagnostics — none of which are implemented; the underlying managers return hardcoded fixture data.
proposed_fix: Replace 'Automatically updates its knowledge base every hour' with 'GitHub integration is scaffolded; polling loop is not yet implemented.' Replace 'Automated Remediation' with 'Diagnostic scaffolding with planned remediation workflows.' Add a 'Current status' section listing which features are implemented vs planned. Do not delete the document; correct the overclaims in place so the community is not misled.
test_plan: After edit, have a second reviewer verify each bullet in the announcement maps to a real code path or is marked as planned. No automated test applies to a markdown doc, but a CI lint step (e.g. markdownlint) should be added to catch future overclaims.

evidence: src/diagnostics_manager.py:110-125 — hardcoded stub returns fixed CrashLoopBackOff data regardless of pod_name; no real k8s API call to detect image pull failures
diagnosis: There is no mechanism to detect, classify, or remediate yanked/missing image tags (e.g., bitnami/kubectl:1.30) because all pod diagnostics return static canned data.
proposed_fix: Data model: ImageTagFinding(pod, namespace, container, current_image, suggested_image, confidence). Interface: async def remediate_image_tag(pod_name, namespace, dry_run) -> RemediationResult. Dependencies: kubernetes python client (already declared in requirements.txt), a registry probe (docker hub or ghcr API). Steps: 1) call core_v1.read_namespaced_pod to get actual image; 2) query registry API for available tags matching semver pattern; 3) if dry_run=False patch the deployment image via apps_v1.patch_namespaced_deployment. Edge cases: multi-container pods, init containers, image digest pinning, private registries requiring auth.
test_plan: Unit-test with a mock k8s client returning a pod spec with bitnami/kubectl:1.30 image; assert remediate_image_tag returns the correct suggested replacement and that patch is called when dry_run=False. Integration test: deploy a pod with a bad tag to a kind cluster and confirm the remediator selects the next available tag.

evidence: src/diagnostics_manager.py:110-148 — comment reads '# Stub implementation - would query actual pod status'; hardcoded status='CrashLoopBackOff', restart_count=5, logs with timestamp '2025-08-09', events with timestamp '2025-08-09T10:29:00Z'
diagnosis: troubleshoot_pod_issues always returns the same fabricated CrashLoopBackOff diagnosis with hardcoded log lines regardless of which pod is queried.
proposed_fix: Replace stub block (lines 111-148) with real k8s calls: v1 = client.CoreV1Api(); pod = v1.read_namespaced_pod(name=pod_name, namespace=namespace); derive status from pod.status.phase and pod.status.container_statuses; fetch logs via v1.read_namespaced_pod_log; fetch events via v1.list_namespaced_event with field_selector=f'involvedObject.name={pod_name}'. Return structured real data.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api with a pod in Pending state; assert the tool returns status=Pending and no CrashLoopBackOff hardcode. Integration test against a live kind cluster with a real failing pod.

evidence: push-and-deploy.sh:3 — 'set -e' only; missing '-u' (unbound var protection) and '-o pipefail' (pipe failure propagation). Line 8 — hardcoded registry path 'ghcr.io/hawaiideveloper/mcp-kubernetes-platform-engineer:latest' with no version tag, overwriting latest silently. No registry login step; if docker push fails after kubectl apply, deployment is left pointing at a broken image.
diagnosis: The deploy script lacks pipefail/unbound-var protection, always pushes mutable 'latest' tag (no immutable digest or version), and has no rollback on partial failure.
proposed_fix: Line 3: change to 'set -euo pipefail'. Add IMAGE_TAG variable derived from git SHA: IMAGE_TAG=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD). Replace 'latest' with $IMAGE_TAG on lines 8 and 9. Add 'docker login ghcr.io' check before push. After kubectl apply, on failure execute 'kubectl rollout undo deployment/kubernetes-mcp-server -n mcp-kubernetes' as rollback.
test_plan: Run script with IMAGE_TAG overridden to a nonexistent image; confirm kubectl rollout undo is invoked. Run shellcheck against the updated script and verify exit 1 on any failed command in a pipe chain.

evidence: k8s/service.yaml:37-47 — second Service is type: NodePort with nodePort: 30001 exposed cluster-wide; no NetworkPolicy restricts access to this port. Also, no appProtocol field on port definitions; prometheus scrape annotation (line 9) points to port 8080 but there is no evidence a /metrics endpoint is implemented.
diagnosis: NodePort service exposes the MCP server directly on every node's external interface without any access restriction, and the prometheus scrape annotation references an unimplemented metrics endpoint.
proposed_fix: Remove or restrict the NodePort service: either delete it and rely on ClusterIP + kubectl port-forward for dev access, or add a NetworkPolicy that whitelists only monitoring-namespace ingress to port 30001. Add appProtocol: http to port entries. Remove prometheus scrape annotations until a /metrics handler is actually implemented in the server.
test_plan: Apply the manifest to a kind cluster and verify no external port is exposed via 'kubectl get svc -n mcp-kubernetes' and nmap scan from outside the cluster. Confirm prometheus scrape annotation is absent until /metrics is implemented.

evidence: src/documentation_manager.py:44-48 — initialize() calls _build_documentation_index() which makes live HTTP requests to kubernetes.io during server startup (line 58-64); if the site is unreachable the entire server fails to start (line 49: raise). Line 269 — _index_linked_pages limits to 10 links per page but recurses without depth limit, risking unbounded crawl.
diagnosis: Server startup is coupled to live network requests with no offline fallback, making the server undeployable in air-gapped or network-restricted environments; crawl depth is uncapped.
proposed_fix: Wrap _build_documentation_index in try/except with a fallback to an empty documentation_db and log a warning instead of raising. Add a max_depth parameter (default 1) to _index_linked_pages and pass depth-1 on recursive calls, stopping at depth==0. Consider pre-bundling a static documentation snapshot as a JSON file shipped with the image.
test_plan: Unit test: mock aiohttp to raise ConnectionError; assert initialize() completes without raising and documentation_db is empty. Test search_documentation returns [] gracefully in that state. Verify depth cap by asserting _fetch_documentation_section is called at most 10+1 times per base URL.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:457-499 defines Tool(name='upgrade_helm_chart',...) in get_enhanced_helm_tools(). Grep of src/mcp_server.py shows zero imports of enhanced_tools and zero references to 'upgrade_helm_chart' in the call_tool dispatch. src/helm_manager.py:141 implements async def upgrade_helm_chart() which calls helm subprocess correctly.
diagnosis: The upgrade_helm_chart tool schema exists in enhanced_tools.py and the handler exists in helm_manager.py, but enhanced_tools is never imported by mcp_server.py so the tool is never registered and never reachable by any MCP client.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py: 1) add 'from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_helm_tools, get_enhanced_kubectl_tools' to imports. 2) In handle_list_tools, extend the tools list: '...+ get_enhanced_helm_tools() + get_enhanced_kubectl_tools()'. 3) In handle_call_tool, add elif branch: 'elif name == "upgrade_helm_chart": result = await self.helm_manager.upgrade_helm_chart(**arguments)'. Mirror for all other helm/kubectl tool names.
test_plan: Start the server, call list_tools via MCP protocol and assert upgrade_helm_chart appears in the response. Call upgrade_helm_chart with dry_run=True against a test release and assert the helm subprocess is invoked with '--dry-run'.

evidence: src/github_issues_manager.py:573-618 implements get_issue_statistics() with real SQLite queries (no stub). mcp_server.py:605-606 dispatches to self.github_issues_manager.get_issue_statistics(). However, github_issues_manager.py:65-66 calls _fetch_recent_issues() during initialize() without a GitHub token (line 56: self.github_token = None) so on first run all 17 repos will hit the unauthenticated GitHub API rate limit (60 req/hr) and statistics will reflect an empty or partial database with no error surfaced to the caller.
diagnosis: get_issue_statistics silently returns zeros/empty dicts when the database was never populated due to GitHub API rate limiting at startup, giving callers false confidence the data is authoritative.
proposed_fix: Add a 'data_quality' field to the statistics response: include 'indexed_repos', 'last_updated', 'rate_limited_repos' so callers can assess freshness. Read GITHUB_TOKEN from env in __init__ (line 56): self.github_token = os.getenv('GITHUB_TOKEN'). In _fetch_issues_page, on 403 append repo to a rate_limited list and persist it so get_issue_statistics can report it.
test_plan: Mock the GitHub API to return 403 for all repos; assert get_issue_statistics returns {'total_issues': 0, 'rate_limited_repos': [...17 repos...]}. With a valid GITHUB_TOKEN mock returning 2 issues, assert total_issues=2 and rate_limited_repos=[].

evidence: No file in the repo implements PR generation. src/mcp_server.py tool list (lines 88-479) has no 'create_pr' or 'open_pr' tool. k8s_manager, diagnostics_manager, monitoring_manager, security_manager all return data but there is no downstream step that writes a git branch, commits a manifest change, or calls the GitHub API to open a PR.
diagnosis: GitOps PR generation is completely absent from the codebase; no data model, no interface, no GitHub API integration.
proposed_fix: Data model: GitOpsPR(finding_id: str, evidence: str, sandbox_log: str, manifest_diff: str, branch: str, pr_url: str). Interface: async def generate_gitops_pr(finding: Finding, sandbox_result: SandboxResult, repo_path: str, github_token: str) -> GitOpsPR. Steps: 1) git checkout -b fix/{finding_id}; 2) write patched manifest; 3) git commit -m 'fix({finding_id}): {one-line-summary}'; 4) git push origin; 5) POST to GitHub API /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls with body containing finding_id, evidence quote, and sandbox_log. Labels: ['auto-remediation', severity_label]. Title format: 'fix({finding_id}): {short_desc}' max 70 chars.
test_plan: Mock git subprocess and GitHub API; assert branch name matches pattern 'fix/{finding_id}', PR title is under 70 chars, body contains finding_id, evidence verbatim, and sandbox log. Assert the 'auto-remediation' label is included in the payload.

evidence: logs.sh:1-3 — script is 3 lines with no shebang validation, no 'set -e', no check that container 'k8s-mcp-server' exists before running 'docker logs'. If the container does not exist, docker logs exits 1 but the absence of set -e means no error propagation. The container name is hardcoded and will silently produce no output if the deployment uses a different name.
diagnosis: logs.sh has no error handling, no pipefail, and a hardcoded container name that will silently fail if the container is not running or has a different name.
proposed_fix: Add '#!/bin/bash' and 'set -euo pipefail' at top. Add a CONTAINER_NAME variable defaulting to 'k8s-mcp-server' but overridable by env var. Add existence check: 'docker inspect "$CONTAINER_NAME" > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Container $CONTAINER_NAME not found"; exit 1; }'. Pass optional '--tail' argument from $1 with a default of 100.
test_plan: Run the updated script with a non-existent container name and assert exit code 1 with error message. Run with CONTAINER_NAME overridden to a real running container and assert logs appear. Run shellcheck against the script with no warnings.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:503-526 defines Tool(name='uninstall_helm_chart',...) in get_enhanced_helm_tools(). src/helm_manager.py:171-192 implements async def uninstall_helm_chart(). No import of enhanced_tools in src/mcp_server.py (confirmed by grep returning no output). No 'uninstall_helm_chart' in mcp_server.py call_tool dispatch.
diagnosis: uninstall_helm_chart is defined in enhanced_tools.py and implemented in helm_manager.py but is never registered or dispatched in mcp_server.py, making it completely unreachable.
proposed_fix: Same wire-up as helm_upgrade: import get_enhanced_helm_tools in mcp_server.py, extend handle_list_tools, and add dispatch in handle_call_tool: 'elif name == "uninstall_helm_chart": if self.non_destructive_mode: result = {"error": "Disabled in non-destructive mode"}; else: result = await self.helm_manager.uninstall_helm_chart(release_name=arguments["release_name"], namespace=arguments.get("namespace"), keep_history=arguments.get("keep_history", False))'.
test_plan: Start server with ALLOW_ONLY_NON_DESTRUCTIVE_TOOLS=true, call uninstall_helm_chart and assert the error response is returned without invoking helm subprocess. With non-destructive mode off and helm mocked, assert helm uninstall is invoked with the correct release name.

evidence: No file named analyzer_base.py exists anywhere in src/; grep for 'BaseAnalyzer' returns zero hits across the entire repo.
diagnosis: The BaseAnalyzer abstract class does not exist; every manager returns ad-hoc dicts with no shared contract, making a future auto-remediator impossible to plug in uniformly.
test_plan: Unit-test that each concrete manager subclass raises TypeError if analyze() is not implemented; integration test that analyze() returns a list[Finding] with all required fields populated for a mock k8s_state.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:605-606 dispatches to github_issues_manager.get_issue_statistics(); src/github_issues_manager.py:573-618 queries a real aiosqlite DB seeded by _fetch_recent_issues(). However github_issues_manager.py:56 sets self.github_token = None, so without a token all GitHub API calls are rate-limited to 60 req/hr and typically fail silently (line 267: 'self.logger.warning(f"Rate limited for {repo}")'). On a cold start the DB is empty and the tool returns all-zero stats with no indication of staleness.
diagnosis: get_issue_statistics returns real SQL aggregates but those aggregates are over an empty or stale database whenever no GitHub token is configured, giving the caller misleading zero-counts with no staleness warning.
proposed_fix:
Add a 'last_fetched_at' timestamp column to the DB; in get_issue_statistics() include it in the returned dict. Add a 'data_ready' boolean that is False when total_issues==0. Surface a 'token_configured' field so callers can tell why data may be absent.
  stats['data_ready'] = stats['total_issues'] > 0
  stats['token_configured'] = bool(self.github_token)
  stats['last_fetched_at'] = <query max(indexed_at)>
test_plan: Test with empty DB: assert data_ready==False and total_issues==0. Test with populated DB: assert data_ready==True and by_state/by_severity dicts are non-empty. Test that token_configured reflects the presence or absence of self.github_token.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:130-159 defines the kubectl_logs Tool schema. grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' returns zero hits — the module is never imported. The handle_call_tool dispatcher (mcp_server.py:483-621) has no case for 'kubectl_logs'. The tool is advertised to MCP clients via handle_list_tools only if wired up, which it is not.
diagnosis: enhanced_tools.py is a dead module: its Tool schemas are never returned to MCP clients and calling any of its tools would fall through to the 'Unknown tool' branch at mcp_server.py:608.
proposed_fix:
In mcp_server.py:
  from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools, get_enhanced_helm_tools
  # In handle_list_tools, extend the tools list:
  tools=[ ...existing... ] + get_enhanced_kubectl_tools() + get_enhanced_helm_tools()
  # In handle_call_tool, add dispatch branches:
  elif name == 'kubectl_logs':
      result = await self.kubectl_manager.kubectl_logs(**arguments)
test_plan: Call handle_list_tools and assert 'kubectl_logs' is present. Call handle_call_tool with name='kubectl_logs' and a valid pod_name; assert result is not the 'Unknown tool' error string and delegates to kubectl_manager.kubectl_logs.

evidence: mcp_server.py:483 — handle_call_tool catches all exceptions and returns them as plain text (line 618-620: 'return CallToolResult(content=[TextContent(type="text", text=f"Error: {str(e)}")])'). Line 484: 'arguments: Optional[Dict[str, Any]]' — if a required field like pod_name is absent, line 502 raises KeyError which is swallowed as a generic 'Error: pod_name'. Lines 59-63 import os inside __init__ instead of at module top. No validation of arguments before passing to managers.
diagnosis: The single bare except clause hides all failures as opaque text strings, making programmatic error handling by MCP clients impossible, and the absence of argument validation means missing required fields produce uninformative KeyError messages.
test_plan: Call handle_call_tool('troubleshoot_pod_issues', {}) — assert isError=True and message mentions 'pod_name'. Call with a valid pod_name against a mocked diagnostics_manager — assert successful result. Verify no bare 'import os' inside __init__.

evidence: github_issues_manager.py:488 builds a raw SQL fragment by interpolating key_terms directly into the query string: 'f"title LIKE \'%{term}%\' OR body LIKE \'%{term}%\'"'. These terms come from user-supplied error_message via _extract_key_terms (line 518). Line 490 executes this raw string with aiosqlite. Additionally, lines 68-72 in initialize() call asyncio.create_task() without storing a reference in a way that allows cleanup on error, and line 72 stores it in self.update_task but the shutdown path at line 225 (stop()) is never called by mcp_server.py.
diagnosis: find_similar_issues constructs SQL with unsanitized user input, exposing a SQL injection vector; the background asyncio task started in initialize() leaks because mcp_server.py never calls stop() on shutdown.
proposed_fix:
Replace the interpolated SQL fragment with parameterized queries:
  placeholders = ' OR '.join(['(title LIKE ? OR body LIKE ?)' for _ in key_terms])
  params = [v for t in key_terms for v in (f'%{t}%', f'%{t}%')]
  sql = f'SELECT ... WHERE ({placeholders}) ...' 
  cursor.execute(sql, params + [max_results])
Add cleanup in mcp_server.py start() finally block: await self.github_issues_manager.stop()
test_plan: Pass error_message="' OR '1'='1" and assert no rows are returned that shouldn't match. Assert the background task is cancelled when stop() is called. Integration test: search for a real term inserted into test DB and confirm expected row is returned.

evidence: CHANGELOG.md:103-104 claims '390 Total Tests' and 'Production-ready functional unit test suite covering all 45,720+ Kubernetes GitHub issues'. Actual test count across all test files is 55 (grep -c 'def test_' across all .py files). CHANGELOG.md:275-284 shows curl responses like '{"status":"secure","vulnerabilities":"None"}' implying a live HTTP REST API; the current server is stdio-only (mcp_server.py:640: 'async with stdio_server()'). CHANGELOG.md:379 claims '99.9% uptime since deployment' — no uptime tracking code exists in the repo.
diagnosis: The CHANGELOG makes multiple materially false production-readiness claims: test count is 7x overclaimed (390 vs 55), a REST API is described that does not exist in the codebase, and operational metrics (uptime, response time) are invented.
proposed_fix:
Replace overclaimed sections with accurate statements:
  - Tests: '55 test functions across 5 files (unit, production, integration stubs)'
  - Server: 'stdio MCP transport only; no HTTP REST API'
  - Remove all 'uptime', 'response time', and 'zero critical issues' operational claims
  - Mark 'Phase 3: Production Readiness' as IN PROGRESS, not COMPLETE
test_plan: Run 'pytest --collect-only -q' and confirm count matches what the changelog states. CI lint step that greps CHANGELOG for absolute claims like '99.9%' and fails the build if present without a linked evidence artifact.

evidence: TEST_SUITE_IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md:9 states '390 Total Tests'. Actual grep count is 55. Line 14 claims '78 Dependencies' — requirements-test.txt does not exist in the repo (find returns nothing). Line 30 states 'conftest.py (446 lines)' — actual file is at tests/conftest.py; wc -l shows it to be present but the Integration, Performance, and Security test directories listed on lines 35-36 do not exist under tests/ (only 'unit' and 'production' subdirs exist). Line 155 states 'Status: COMPLETE - Production-Ready Test Suite Successfully Implemented'.
diagnosis: TEST_SUITE_IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md fabricates test counts, dependency counts, directory structure, and completion status that are contradicted by the actual repository contents.
proposed_fix:
Rewrite the summary to reflect reality:
  - Total tests: 55 (counted via pytest --collect-only)
  - Test categories present: unit (21), production (35); integration/performance/security missing
  - requirements-test.txt: not present
  - Status: PARTIAL — unit and production stubs exist; integration, performance, security suites are unimplemented
  Remove all coverage percentage and 45,720+ issue coverage claims.
test_plan: Add a CI job that runs 'pytest --collect-only -q 2>&1 | tail -1' and writes the count to a badge file; gate merges on that count matching a documented minimum.

evidence: No file, class, or function named 'dpo', 'dpo_pair', or 'DPO' exists anywhere in the repo (grep returns zero hits). The concept of DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) training pair generation is entirely absent from the codebase.
diagnosis: DPO pair extraction behavior is undesigned and unimplemented; no data model, interface, or storage layer exists for it.
proposed_fix:
Data model:
  @dataclass
  class DPOPair:
      prompt: str          # the k8s incident description
      chosen: str          # accepted remediation action + evidence
      rejected: list[str]  # alternative actions that were not taken
      metadata: dict       # issue_id, severity, component, timestamp
Interface: async def extract_dpo_pair(issue: GitHubIssue, chosen_action: str, rejected_actions: list[str]) -> DPOPair
Storage: append to JSONL file or insert into a dpo_pairs SQLite table alongside github_issues.db
Edge cases: issue body is None, chosen_action duplicated in rejected list, non-English body text.
test_plan: Unit test: given a mock issue with body text, chosen='restart pod', rejected=['delete pod', 'ignore'], assert returned DPOPair has prompt derived from title+body and chosen/rejected correctly assigned. Test edge cases: None body, empty rejected list.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:560-563 dispatches to documentation_manager.search_documentation(). src/documentation_manager.py:51-88 builds documentation_db by making live HTTP GET requests to kubernetes.io during initialize(). If network is unavailable or rate-limited, line 87 logs the error and continues, leaving documentation_db empty. search_documentation at line 336 then iterates over an empty dict and silently returns []. No cache persistence to disk; every server restart re-crawls from scratch. The tool description claims 'Search Kubernetes official documentation' but may silently return nothing.
diagnosis: search_documentation silently returns empty results when the live crawl fails at startup, with no indication to the caller that the documentation index is empty; the index is also never persisted so restarts always re-crawl.
test_plan: Mock aiohttp to raise an exception; call initialize() and then search_documentation('pod'); assert result metadata shows index_size==0 rather than silent empty list. After a successful mock crawl, assert cache file is written and reloaded on next initialize() without network calls.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:500-506 dispatches to diagnostics_manager.troubleshoot_pod_issues(). src/diagnostics_manager.py:110-148 contains the implementation: line 111 has the comment '# Stub implementation - would query actual pod status', lines 112-148 unconditionally return hardcoded data: status='CrashLoopBackOff', restart_count=5, logs=['2025-08-09 10:30:15 ERROR: Failed to connect to database', ...]. No kubernetes client call is made regardless of the pod_name or namespace passed.
diagnosis: troubleshoot_pod_issues always returns the same hardcoded CrashLoopBackOff scenario regardless of the actual pod requested, making it useless and actively misleading for real cluster troubleshooting.
proposed_fix:
Replace stub with real kubernetes client calls:
  from kubernetes_asyncio import client, config
  v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
  pod = await v1.read_namespaced_pod(name=pod_name, namespace=namespace)
  results['status'] = pod.status.phase
  results['restart_count'] = sum(cs.restart_count for cs in (pod.status.container_statuses or []))
  if include_logs:
      results['logs'] = await v1.read_namespaced_pod_log(name=pod_name, namespace=namespace, tail_lines=50)
  if include_events:
      events = await v1.list_namespaced_event(namespace, field_selector=f'involvedObject.name={pod_name}')
      results['events'] = [{'type':e.type,'reason':e.reason,'message':e.message} for e in events.items]
test_plan: Mock kubernetes_asyncio client to return a pod with phase='Running' and zero restarts; assert results['status']=='Running' and results['restart_count']==0. Mock a pod with CrashLoopBackOff state; assert matching output. Test that passing a non-existent pod_name raises ApiException which is caught and returned as an error dict.

evidence: health_check.py:43-45 — `import asyncio; import json; import logging; return True`
diagnosis: check_imports() only verifies stdlib modules that cannot fail to import, so the health check never detects missing critical dependencies like `kubernetes`, `mcp`, `aiohttp`, or `beautifulsoup4`.
proposed_fix: Replace check_imports with: try: import kubernetes; import mcp; import aiohttp; from bs4 import BeautifulSoup; return True except ImportError as e: logger.error(f'Missing dependency: {e}'); return False. Also add a k8s connectivity probe: attempt kubernetes.config.load_incluster_config() or load_kube_config() and call CoreV1Api().list_namespace(limit=1) to confirm live cluster access.
test_plan: Run health_check.check() in a venv missing the `kubernetes` package and assert it returns False. Run it in a fully-wired environment with a live cluster and assert True. Verify sys.exit codes match.

evidence: kubectl_manager.py:45-81 — `_run_kubectl` is synchronous `subprocess.run` called from async methods like `kubectl_get` (line 119) without `asyncio.to_thread` or `loop.run_in_executor`.
diagnosis: All kubectl operations block the asyncio event loop because `subprocess.run` is called directly from coroutines, which will stall every other MCP tool call for up to 60 seconds (the timeout).
proposed_fix: Change `_run_kubectl` to an async method: `async def _run_kubectl(self, args, input_data=None)` and replace `subprocess.run(...)` with `await asyncio.to_thread(subprocess.run, cmd, ...)`. All callers already use `await`, so no signature changes are needed for public methods.
test_plan: Write an async test that fires two concurrent kubectl_get calls against a mock that sleeps 0.5 s; measure wall time — it should be ~0.5 s not ~1.0 s. Assert return type is still Dict[str, Any].

evidence: status.sh:1 — `#!/bin/bash` with no `set -euo pipefail`; status.sh:3 — relies solely on `docker ps | grep -q k8s-mcp-server` for health assessment.
diagnosis: The script lacks `set -euo pipefail` so errors in any pipeline step are silently swallowed; it also reports a fixed 'Health: OK' string (line 4) regardless of actual application health, providing false confidence.
proposed_fix: Add `set -euo pipefail` on line 2. Replace the hardcoded 'Health: OK' on line 4 with an actual health probe: `docker exec k8s-mcp-server python src/health_check.py 2>&1 && echo '\u2713 Health: OK' || echo '\u2717 Health: FAILED'`. Add exit code propagation so CI can detect failures.
test_plan: Run the script when the container is absent and assert exit code is non-zero. Run it with a running but unhealthy container (health_check.py returns 1) and assert 'Health: FAILED' appears in output.

evidence: github_issues_manager.py:56 — `self.github_token = None`; github_issues_manager.py:154-155 — `if self.github_token: headers['Authorization'] = f'token {self.github_token}'`
diagnosis: The GitHub token is always None (never read from config or environment), so every request runs unauthenticated at GitHub's 60 req/hr rate limit, causing rate-limit failures (403) that are silently swallowed during the background scrape of 17 repos.
proposed_fix: In `__init__`, replace `self.github_token = None` with `self.github_token = getattr(config, 'github_token', None) or os.getenv('GITHUB_TOKEN')`. Add `github_token: Optional[str] = None` to `ServerConfig`. Document in README that GITHUB_TOKEN env var is required for reliable operation.
test_plan: Mock aiohttp.ClientSession and assert the Authorization header is set when GITHUB_TOKEN env var is present. Assert requests succeed and issues are stored in SQLite. Verify 403 responses log a warning and do not raise.

evidence: mcp_server.py:515-521 — dispatches to `self.diagnostics_manager.check_network_connectivity`; diagnostics_manager.py:185-210 — `# Stub implementation for different test types` returns hardcoded latency/IP values (latency_ms: 2.3, resolved_ip: '10.96.0.1') regardless of inputs.
diagnosis: The tool handler exists and routes correctly, but the implementation is an explicit stub that returns fabricated connectivity data instead of executing any real probe (kubectl exec, nslookup, curl) against the cluster.
proposed_fix: Replace stub branches with real kubectl exec calls: `kubectl exec <source_pod> -n <namespace> -- ping -c 3 <target>` for ping, `kubectl exec ... -- nslookup <target>` for dns, `kubectl exec ... -- curl -sI <target>` for http. Parse stdout/stderr into the result dict. Return actual exit codes and timings.
test_plan: Integration test against a real cluster: assert resolved_ip matches actual CoreDNS response, not the hardcoded '10.96.0.1'. Unit test: mock subprocess output and verify parser produces correct status/latency fields.

evidence: test_issue_patterns.py:52-53 — `github_issues_manager.analyze_issue_pattern = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_result)` then line 55 — `result = await github_issues_manager.analyze_issue_pattern(pattern)` — every async test in the file sets the mock and immediately calls it.
diagnosis: All 18 async tests are tautological: each test overwrites the method with an AsyncMock that returns a hardcoded dict, then asserts fields from that same dict — no real code path in GitHubIssuesManager is exercised.
proposed_fix: Remove the AsyncMock overwrite pattern. Tests for `_analyze_severity` and `_analyze_component` (lines 370-431) are sync and call the real methods — replicate that pattern for all tests. For `analyze_issue_pattern` (which does not yet exist on the real class), add a conftest fixture that prepopulates the SQLite DB with known issues, then call the real `search_issues` or `find_similar_issues` methods and assert on actual returned data.
test_plan: After rewrite, run pytest with `--no-header -q`; confirm no test passes solely because it mocks its own subject. Measure coverage with `pytest --cov=src/github_issues_manager` and require >60% line coverage.

evidence: documentation_manager.py:36 — `self.documentation_db = {}` initialized empty; documentation_manager.py:328-373 — `search_documentation` iterates `self.documentation_db` which is populated only if the live HTTP scrape in `_build_documentation_index` succeeds at startup.
diagnosis: The in-memory `documentation_db` dict is not persisted, so every server restart triggers a full live scrape of kubernetes.io; if the scrape fails or the server starts offline, all search and command-lookup tools silently return empty lists.
proposed_fix: Add an SQLite or JSON-file cache (analogous to github_issues_manager). On `initialize()`, check if cache is fresh (< 24 h old) and load from disk; only re-scrape when stale. Fall back to the hardcoded `best_practices` dict when `documentation_db` is empty so tools remain functional offline.
test_plan: Unit test: pre-populate the cache file with a synthetic doc entry, call `search_documentation('pod')` without network, and assert results are returned. Integration test: run `initialize()` with mocked aiohttp and verify cache file is written.

evidence: No cache module exists in src/; LLM explanation calls (if added) would re-invoke the model on every identical finding.
diagnosis: Design task: specify a finding-hash-to-LLM-explanation cache with TTL so repeated identical findings are served from cache rather than re-invoking the LLM.
proposed_fix: Data model: SQLite table `explanation_cache(hash TEXT PRIMARY KEY, finding_json TEXT, explanation TEXT, created_at TEXT, expires_at TEXT)`. Hash key: SHA-256 of normalized finding dict (sorted keys, stripped whitespace). Interface: `async def get_or_generate(finding: dict, ttl_seconds: int = 86400) -> str` in a new `src/explanation_cache.py`. TTL enforced at read time: if `expires_at < now` treat as miss. Edge cases: concurrent cache misses for same hash (use asyncio.Lock keyed by hash to prevent thundering herd); cache invalidation on model version bump (store model_id in row, reject hits on mismatch).
test_plan: Test 1: call get_or_generate twice with same finding dict, assert LLM stub called exactly once. Test 2: insert a row with expires_at in the past, assert cache miss and re-generation. Test 3: two concurrent coroutines request same hash, assert LLM stub called exactly once (lock test).

evidence: mcp_server.py:546-549 — dispatches to `self.k8s_manager.get_recommendations`; k8s_manager.py:105-160 — returns a static dict of hardcoded recommendation strings; no k8s API call is made; `cluster_context` parameter (line 91) is accepted but never used.
diagnosis: The tool routes correctly but the handler returns canned generic advice that is not derived from actual cluster state, making the tool useless for real cluster optimization.
proposed_fix: Implement real analysis: call `CoreV1Api().list_pod_for_all_namespaces()` and `AppsV1Api().list_deployment_for_all_namespaces()`, then inspect `.spec.containers[].resources` for missing limits/requests (performance focus), `.spec.securityContext` for missing runAsNonRoot (security focus), and `.spec.replicas` == 1 with no PDB (reliability focus). Build recommendations from actual findings rather than hardcoded strings. Pass `cluster_context` through as filter criteria.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes client to return a deployment with no resource limits; assert the returned recommendations list contains a resource-limits entry. Mock a deployment with runAsRoot=True; assert a security recommendation is returned. Assert empty recommendations when cluster is fully compliant.

evidence: security_manager.py:92-116 (_scan_rbac), 118-150 (_scan_pod_security), 152-176 (_scan_network_policies), 178-201 (_scan_secrets), 203-232 (_scan_container_images) — every private scan method is a hardcoded return dict with fabricated counts (e.g., `'total_pods': 45`, `'total_secrets': 12`, specific CVE strings like `'CVE-2021-23017'`) and never calls any k8s API or image scanner.
diagnosis: The entire security_scan surface (5 scan types) returns plausible-looking but entirely fabricated data, meaning users acting on security scan results may believe their cluster is secure when it is not.
proposed_fix: Replace each stub with real k8s API calls: `_scan_rbac` -> list ClusterRoleBindings via `RbacAuthorizationV1Api` and flag subjects with cluster-admin. `_scan_pod_security` -> iterate pods via `CoreV1Api().list_pod_for_all_namespaces()`, check `.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged`. `_scan_secrets` -> list secrets and flag those with no associated serviceaccount reference. `_scan_container_images` -> extract image names and call an external scanner (Trivy HTTP API or subprocess). `_scan_network_policies` -> list NetworkPolicy objects per namespace.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes client returning a pod with `privileged: true`; assert `_scan_pod_security` returns that pod in issues list with severity=high. Mock an empty NetworkPolicy list for a namespace; assert `_scan_network_policies` flags it as missing_network_policy. Confirm no hardcoded resource names appear in output.

evidence: No SQLite worklist file exists anywhere in the repo — confirmed by absence in src/, data/, and root.
diagnosis: There is no atomic claim/release mechanism; parallel remediator agents would collide on the same work items with no locking.
proposed_fix: Create src/worklist_db.py with table: worklist(id INTEGER PK, component_id TEXT UNIQUE, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('pending','claimed','done','failed')), claimed_by TEXT, claimed_at TEXT, released_at TEXT). Expose: async def claim_item(agent_id) -> Optional[str] using BEGIN IMMEDIATE + UPDATE WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1 + RETURNING inside a single transaction to guarantee atomicity. Also expose release_item(component_id, agent_id, new_status) and seed_worklist(components: List[str]).
test_plan: Spin 10 concurrent asyncio tasks all calling claim_item(); assert each returns a distinct component_id with no duplicates. Then call release_item() with status='done' and assert the item is no longer claimable.

evidence: No audit log file, table, or logger exists in the repo for remediator actions — confirmed by grep across src/.
diagnosis: No immutable audit trail of who-did-what-when exists, making post-incident review and compliance impossible.
proposed_fix: Create src/audit_log.py with SQLite table: audit_log(id INTEGER PK, ts TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT(datetime('now')), agent_id TEXT, action TEXT, component_id TEXT, evidence TEXT, outcome TEXT CHECK(outcome IN ('success','failure','skipped')), detail TEXT). Expose: async def append(agent_id, action, component_id, evidence, outcome, detail='') which does a single INSERT — never UPDATE/DELETE. Wire into every remediator action with a try/finally so failures are also logged.
test_plan: Call append() 5 times with varying outcomes; assert all 5 rows exist with correct fields. Simulate a crash mid-append and assert previously written rows are intact (WAL mode).

evidence: monitoring_manager.py:55 — `"timestamp": "2025-08-09T10:30:00Z"` (hardcoded); line 99 — `"total_entries": 1247`; line 107-128 — entire log_summary and top_errors are static dicts; lines 196-254 — all _analyze_*_resources methods return hardcoded values with no kubernetes client calls.
diagnosis: Every public method in MonitoringManager returns fabricated static data; no kubernetes client, metrics-server, or log aggregator is queried at any point.
proposed_fix: Inject a kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api and CustomObjectsApi (metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1) in __init__. In analyze_resource_usage: call custom_objects_api.list_cluster_custom_object('metrics.k8s.io','v1beta1','nodes') for node metrics and equivalent pods endpoint. In analyze_logs: call core_v1_api.read_namespaced_pod_log(pod_name, namespace, since_seconds=...) and parse results. Replace all hardcoded timestamps with datetime.utcnow().isoformat().
test_plan: Integration test with a real or kind cluster: assert returned cpu/memory values match kubectl top output within 5%. Unit test with mocked k8s client: assert client methods are called with correct args and no hardcoded data leaks through.

evidence: mcp_server.py:489-492 dispatches to k8s_manager.get_cluster_info(). k8s_manager.py:22-23 — `self.client = None; self.api_client = None`. k8s_manager.py:29-31 — initialize() logs a message and returns without calling kubernetes.config.load_incluster_config() or load_kube_config(). k8s_manager.py:50-74 — get_cluster_info() returns fully hardcoded dict including `"version": "v1.29.0"`, `"total": 3` nodes, `"total": 45` pods.
diagnosis: get_cluster_info never contacts a real cluster; it returns a fabricated snapshot regardless of the actual cluster state.
proposed_fix: In KubernetesManager.initialize(): call kubernetes.config.load_incluster_config() with fallback to load_kube_config(); assign self.core_v1 = kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api() and self.version_api = kubernetes.client.VersionApi(). In get_cluster_info(): call self.version_api.get_code() for version, self.core_v1.list_node() for node counts, self.core_v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces() for pod counts. Handle ApiException with a clear error message.
test_plan: Run against a live kind cluster; assert returned version matches `kubectl version --short`. Mock the k8s client in unit tests and assert all three API methods are invoked.

evidence: configmap.yaml:1-19 — ConfigMap contains only string key-value pairs. No Secret reference for sensitive values. Keys RBAC_ENABLED, POD_SECURITY_STANDARDS, NETWORK_POLICIES are present but the application code (src/config.py) is not shown to consume them, and no validation that their values affect behavior exists.
diagnosis: ConfigMap places no sensitive data in Secrets and provides no immutable flag; any pod with read access to the namespace can read all configuration, and stale values after a deploy are silently ignored.
proposed_fix: Add `immutable: true` to prevent accidental edits after deploy. Add a comment block documenting which env vars are consumed by the app. Move any future sensitive config (e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN) to a Secret referenced via envFrom.secretRef rather than a ConfigMap key. Verify each key maps to a field in src/config.py; remove dead keys.
test_plan: Apply the updated ConfigMap and confirm `kubectl get cm kubernetes-mcp-server-config -o yaml` shows immutable: true. Confirm the app pod starts without error and logs show the expected config values.

evidence: mcp_server.py:583-591 dispatches to github_issues_manager.search_issues(). github_issues_manager.py:410-460 — search_issues() executes a real SQLite query against self.db_path. However, github_issues_manager.py:56 — `self.github_token = None` (hardcoded None); lines 154-155 — token is only added to headers if truthy. GitHub unauthenticated rate limit is 60 req/hr; the repo list at lines 37-55 contains 17 repos, so a single initialize() call exhausts the rate limit.
diagnosis: search_github_issues queries a real SQLite DB populated via the GitHub REST API, but with no token the fetch silently fails (return_exceptions=True at line 165), leaving the DB empty so all searches return empty arrays.
proposed_fix: Read GITHUB_TOKEN from os.environ in __init__ instead of hardcoding None. In _fetch_repo_issues, check the response status: if 403/429, log a warning with rate-limit reset time and skip rather than silently swallowing. Add a startup check: if token is None, log a warning that search_github_issues will return empty results. Expose a health endpoint that reports DB row count.
test_plan: Set GITHUB_TOKEN env var and run integration test; assert DB row count > 0 after initialize(). Unit test _fetch_repo_issues with a mocked 403 response; assert a warning is logged and no exception propagates.

evidence: test_cluster_management.py:108 — `k8s_manager.resolve_node_not_ready = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resolution)`. Line 111 — `resolution = await k8s_manager.resolve_node_not_ready(node_context)`. This pattern is repeated for every test (lines 200-203, 288-291, 382-385, 485-488, 572-575, 666-669, 759-762). The fixture `k8s_manager` is not defined in this file; no conftest.py found in the same directory.
diagnosis: Every test installs an AsyncMock on the method being tested, then calls the mock — it is a tautological assertion that the mock returns what it was told to return; no production code is exercised.
proposed_fix: Replace AsyncMock pattern with a real (or kind-cluster-backed) KubernetesManager instance. Each test should call the actual method with a crafted input and assert on real output shape and content. For offline CI, use responses or a VCR cassette to replay k8s API responses rather than mocking the method under test. Add a conftest.py that provisions a pytest fixture backed by a kind cluster or recorded API responses.
test_plan: After rewrite, run pytest with --tb=short; assert no test patches the method under test. Assert test coverage of k8s_manager.py rises above 70% per coverage report.

evidence: main.py:42-48 — CORSMiddleware configured with `allow_origins=["*"]`. main.py:103-115 — /k8s/diagnostics returns `{"status": "healthy", "cluster_info": "Available", "nodes": "Ready", "pods": "Running"}` as a hardcoded dict without calling any manager. Lines 117-127 and 129-139 — /k8s/monitoring and /k8s/security endpoints share the same pattern. main.py:61 — `self.mcp_server.start()` is never called from K8sPlatformApp; the HTTP server and the MCP stdio server are two disconnected entry points with no shared state.
diagnosis: The HTTP REST endpoints are stubs that return fabricated health data, CORS is open to all origins, and the HTTP server path never invokes the MCP server's stdio transport — the two modes are operationally isolated with no shared manager state.
proposed_fix: Restrict CORSMiddleware allow_origins to an env-var list (default: localhost). Wire /k8s/diagnostics to call self.mcp_server.diagnostics_manager.diagnose_cluster_health() and return real data. Remove the duplicate manager initialization in K8sPlatformApp.initialize() — it duplicates mcp_server.start()'s own init sequence. Document clearly that the stdio entry point (cli stdio) is the MCP path and the HTTP entry point is a monitoring/ops sidecar only.
test_plan: Start the HTTP server against a kind cluster; assert /k8s/diagnostics returns a non-trivial dict with real node names. Assert that an OPTIONS preflight from an arbitrary origin is rejected when ALLOWED_ORIGINS is set to a specific domain.

evidence: rbac.yaml:20 — ClusterRole grants `"get", "list", "watch"` on `"secrets"` cluster-wide. rbac.yaml:47-53 — policy/podsecuritypolicies and extensions/ingresses are included (both deprecated/removed in k8s 1.25+). rbac.yaml:55-69 — ClusterRoleBinding binds the role at cluster scope with no namespace restriction.
diagnosis: The service account has read access to every Secret in the entire cluster — a privilege escalation vector if the pod is compromised — and includes rules for removed API groups that will cause a warning on apply against 1.25+ clusters.
proposed_fix: Remove `secrets` from the cluster-wide ClusterRole. If secret inspection is needed for security_scan, create a separate RoleBinding scoped to `mcp-kubernetes` namespace only for secrets. Remove the `policy` and `extensions` apiGroups blocks (they no longer exist in 1.25+). Consider downgrading from ClusterRole to Role for namespace-scoped operations and only keeping ClusterRole rules for truly cluster-scoped resources (nodes, namespaces, clusterroles).
test_plan: Apply the updated rbac.yaml to a 1.28 cluster; assert `kubectl apply` produces no warnings about deprecated API groups. Run `kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:mcp-kubernetes:kubernetes-mcp-server-sa -n kube-system` and assert it returns 'no'.

evidence: enhanced_tools.py:130-160 — Tool(name='kubectl_logs', ...) is defined with full inputSchema. Grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' or 'kubectl_logs' returns zero results — the module is never imported and the tool schema is never registered with the MCP server.
diagnosis: kubectl_logs is a dead schema: it is defined in enhanced_tools.py but mcp_server.py does not import enhanced_tools, so the tool is never exposed to clients and the handler is never called.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py, add `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools` and extend the list_tools() return with `+ get_enhanced_kubectl_tools()`. In handle_call_tool, add an elif branch for 'kubectl_logs' that calls self.kubectl_manager.get_logs(pod_name=arguments['pod_name'], namespace=arguments.get('namespace','default'), container=arguments.get('container'), tail=arguments.get('tail')). Implement kubectl_manager.get_logs() using core_v1_api.read_namespaced_pod_log().
test_plan: After wiring, call the MCP server with tool name 'kubectl_logs' and a known pod name on a kind cluster; assert the response contains actual log lines. Assert that list_tools() includes kubectl_logs in its output.

evidence: TEST_SUITE_IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md:5 — '390 Total Tests'; TEST_SUITE_IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md:37 — 'Test directories: unit/, integration/, performance/, security/, production/'; actual counts: tests/unit/test_issue_patterns.py:21, tests/production/ files total 34; integration/, performance/, security/ subdirs do not exist
diagnosis: Summary overclaims 390 tests across 5 categories — actual count is 55 test functions across 2 categories; 3 of 5 claimed test directories (integration, performance, security) are absent.
proposed_fix: Update summary to reflect actual counts (55 tests, 2 directories). Remove claims for integration/, performance/, security/ test directories until they are created. Add a 'Status: INCOMPLETE' section listing the missing categories.
test_plan: Run `find tests/ -name '*.py' | xargs grep -c 'def test_'` and compare to documented counts; verify all 5 listed directories exist under tests/.

evidence: deploy-k8s.sh:6 — 'set -e' (only); deploy-k8s.sh:106 — 'read -p "Deploy Ingress for external access? (y/n): " -n 1 -r'; deploy-k8s.sh:132 — 'kubectl get service mcp-server-service' vs k8s/service.yaml:4 — 'name: kubernetes-mcp-server-service'
diagnosis: Script has three distinct defects: missing `set -uo pipefail` allows silent failures on unset variables and broken pipes; interactive `read` breaks CI/non-TTY execution; hardcoded service name 'mcp-server-service' does not match the YAML-defined name 'kubernetes-mcp-server-service', so NodePort and ClusterIP lookups silently return empty strings.
proposed_fix: Line 6: change `set -e` to `set -euo pipefail`. Line 106-112: replace interactive read with a CLI flag `--ingress` defaulting to false. Lines 132-137: change 'mcp-server-service' to 'kubernetes-mcp-server-service' to match k8s/service.yaml. Add `|| true` guard only on the port-forward kill at line 158.
test_plan: Run `bash -n deploy-k8s.sh` for syntax; in a kind cluster run the full script with the service name fix and assert NodePort var is non-empty; confirm CI execution with no TTY does not hang.

evidence: GettingStarted.md:40 — 'kubectl port-forward -n mcp-kubernetes service/kubernetes-mcp-server-service 3001:3001'; deploy-k8s.sh:141 — 'kubectl port-forward -n mcp-kubernetes service/mcp-server-service 3001:3001'; GettingStarted.md:17 — 'curl -fsSL .../start.sh | bash' — start.sh exists but the guide makes no mention of kubeconfig requirement for the Copilot integration to work against a real cluster
diagnosis: GettingStarted.md references the correct service name 'kubernetes-mcp-server-service' while deploy-k8s.sh uses a mismatched name, creating a user-facing inconsistency; the guide also omits the kubeconfig pre-requisite for cluster-connected Copilot queries.
proposed_fix: Add a prerequisites section noting KUBECONFIG must point to a reachable cluster before running Copilot queries. Align service name references across GettingStarted.md and deploy-k8s.sh to the canonical 'kubernetes-mcp-server-service' used in k8s/service.yaml.
test_plan: Follow the guide verbatim on a fresh machine; verify port-forward succeeds and `curl localhost:3001/health` returns 200.

evidence: stop.sh:1 — '#!/bin/bash' with no `set -euo pipefail`; stop.sh:3-4 — errors suppressed via '2>/dev/null || true' on both docker stop and docker rm
diagnosis: stop.sh lacks `set -euo pipefail` and uses blanket error suppression, making it impossible to distinguish a successful stop from a failed one; no logging of which container was stopped.
proposed_fix: Add `set -euo pipefail` after the shebang. Use explicit checks: `if docker ps -q -f name=k8s-mcp-server | grep -q .; then docker stop k8s-mcp-server; docker rm k8s-mcp-server; echo 'Stopped'; else echo 'Container not running'; fi`. Remove blanket `2>/dev/null || true`.
test_plan: Run stop.sh when no container exists and verify exit code is 0 with a clear message. Run when container is running and verify it is removed.

evidence: grep across all src/*.py finds zero calls to autoscaling API (list_namespaced_horizontal_pod_autoscaler, AutoscalingV1Api, AutoscalingV2Api); k8s_manager.py:122 mentions HPA only in a static best-practice string, not in live analysis code
diagnosis: No HPAAnalyzer component exists; no code queries the Kubernetes autoscaling API, so no analysis of HPA metrics exhaustion, maxReplicas cap saturation, or scaling inhibition is possible.
proposed_fix: Create src/hpa_analyzer.py with HPAAnalyzer class. Data model: HPAStatus(name, namespace, current_replicas, desired_replicas, max_replicas, min_replicas, metrics_available, conditions). Interface: `async def analyze(namespace='all') -> List[HPAStatus]`. Uses kubernetes.client.AutoscalingV2Api.list_namespaced_horizontal_pod_autoscaler(). Edge cases: HPA with no metrics source (MetricsUnavailable condition), HPA capped at maxReplicas with pending desired > max, HPA with zero current due to scaledown-cooldown. Emit findings with severity 'critical' when desired==max for >N minutes.
test_plan: Unit test with mocked AutoscalingV2Api returning a capped HPA; assert analyze() returns HPAStatus with a 'capped_at_max' finding. Integration test against a kind cluster with a deployed HPA.

evidence: K8S_ANNOUNCEMENT.md:12 — 'Monitors 17+ major Kubernetes ecosystem repos...Automatically updates its knowledge base every hour'; K8S_ANNOUNCEMENT.md:26 — '1,029+ official Kubernetes reference docs integrated for live search'; src/k8s_manager.py:29-31 — 'Kubernetes client initialization would go here / For now, we'll use a stub implementation'; src/documentation_manager.py:documentation_db is populated only from live HTTP crawls with no persistence, meaning it is empty on startup and lost on restart
diagnosis: The announcement claims production-grade continuous learning, hourly GitHub issue ingestion, and 1,029+ pre-indexed docs, but the underlying code is either stubbed (k8s_manager) or depends on transient HTTP scraping with no on-disk persistence; the 1,029 doc count is unverifiable and the hourly update claim exists only as a sleep loop, not a verified dataset.
proposed_fix: Replace marketing claims with accurate capability statements: 'Attempts hourly GitHub issue ingestion via aiohttp (no persistence; data lost on restart)', 'Documentation index populated at startup from live kubernetes.io scraping (no bundled corpus)', 'Cluster operations return stub data pending kubernetes-client wiring'. Add a roadmap section for production readiness.
test_plan: Restart the server and immediately query search_documentation(); assert documentation_db is empty until _build_documentation_index completes, confirming no pre-seeded corpus exists.

evidence: documentation_manager.py:51-66 — _build_documentation_index() crawls live URLs at startup with no caching or persistence; documentation_manager.py:269 — 'links[:10]' hard-caps crawl to 10 links per page with no explanation; documentation_manager.py:280-326 — _load_best_practices() populates self.best_practices with hardcoded static strings, not live documentation; documentation_manager.py:40-49 — initialize() raises on any fetch error, crashing the whole server if kubernetes.io is unreachable
diagnosis: The documentation manager has three gaps: crawled data is never persisted so it resets on restart; the 10-link cap silently limits coverage far below the claimed 1,029 docs; best_practices is hardcoded and never updated from actual crawl results.
proposed_fix: Add a SQLite/JSON cache (similar to github_issues_manager) for crawled docs with a TTL. Remove the links[:10] hard cap or make it configurable. Separate best_practices loading from the hardcoded dict into a method that enriches from crawled content. Wrap initialize() errors in a degraded-mode path that logs a warning rather than raising, so the server starts without live docs.
test_plan: Confirm that after initialize() with network mocked to fail, search_documentation() returns [] instead of raising. Confirm that after a successful crawl, documentation_db persists across a simulated restart.

evidence: logger.py:12 — 'from rich.console import Console'; logger.py:39 — 'console = Console()' — Console object is constructed but never used (no console.print calls); logger.py:112 — 'def get_logger(name: str) -> logger' — return type annotation is loguru.logger (module), not Logger; main.py:55 — 'self.logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)' bypasses get_logger() and uses stdlib logging directly
diagnosis: logger.py has a dead import+instantiation of Rich Console, an incorrect return type annotation, and main.py does not use get_logger() — meaning stdlib logging and loguru run in parallel with inconsistent formatting.
proposed_fix: Remove the unused `console = Console()` at line 39. Fix return type annotation: `def get_logger(name: str) -> 'loguru.Logger'`. Update main.py lines 55 and 192 to use `from logger import get_logger` and `self.logger = get_logger(__name__)` to enforce a single log path.
test_plan: Run the server and verify all log lines share the same format; assert `isinstance(get_logger('test'), type(loguru.logger))` passes; grep src/ for `logging.getLogger` and confirm only InterceptHandler uses it.

evidence: main.py:44 — 'allow_origins=["*"]'; main.py:45 — 'allow_credentials=True'; main.py:107-114 — /k8s/diagnostics returns hardcoded '{"status": "healthy", "cluster_info": "Available", "nodes": "Ready", "pods": "Running"}'; main.py:117-126 — /k8s/monitoring returns hardcoded '{"status": "monitoring_active", "metrics": "Available", "alerts": "None"}'; main.py:129-138 — /k8s/security returns hardcoded '{"status": "secure", "vulnerabilities": "None"}'
diagnosis: main.py exposes three HTTP endpoints that return hardcoded fake data rather than calling any manager method, and simultaneously sets allow_credentials=True with a wildcard CORS origin — a configuration that browsers reject as invalid and that, if accepted, would allow any origin to send credentialed cross-site requests.
proposed_fix: CORS: set allow_origins to an explicit list (configurable via env var), remove allow_credentials=True or restrict to known origins. Diagnostics endpoint: replace hardcoded dict with `await app_instance.mcp_server.k8s_manager.get_cluster_info()`. Monitoring endpoint: delegate to monitoring_manager. Security endpoint: delegate to security_manager. Add app_instance to FastAPI app state at startup.
test_plan: Confirm browser CORS preflight for a cross-origin credentialed request is rejected before fix, then returns correct headers after fix. Integration test: call /k8s/diagnostics against a live cluster and assert response contains real node count.

evidence: grep across all src/*.py finds no allowlist, namespace whitelist, or auto-merge gate; src/k8s_manager.py:166-210 — execute_remediation() accepts any issue_type and target with only a dry_run flag; no namespace filtering exists
diagnosis: No safety allowlist is implemented; execute_remediation() will attempt destructive operations on any namespace including kube-system, making automated remediation unsafe for production use.
proposed_fix: Add a RemediationPolicy dataclass with fields: auto_merge_namespaces: List[str], pr_only_namespaces: List[str], blocked_operations: List[str]. Load from config (env var K8S_AUTO_MERGE_NS, default ['dev','staging']). In execute_remediation(): if namespace in auto_merge_namespaces and operation not in blocked_operations: execute directly; elif namespace in pr_only_namespaces: return a RemediationProposal object with the planned kubectl commands for human review; else: raise PermissionError. Edge cases: namespace=None (cluster-scoped resources), wildcard namespace patterns, operations that span multiple namespaces.
test_plan: Unit test: call execute_remediation with namespace='kube-system' and assert PermissionError is raised. Call with namespace='dev' (allowlisted) and assert the operation proceeds. Call with namespace='production' (PR-only) and assert a RemediationProposal is returned without executing.

evidence: src/github_issues_manager.py:75-143 — _setup_database creates tables with UNIQUE(repo, issue_number) but no worklist/claim table exists anywhere in the codebase
diagnosis: No agent-claim worklist table or atomic claim/release mechanism exists; concurrent agents reading the same SQLite DB will collide on work units with no coordination primitive.
proposed_fix: Add a `worklist` table: CREATE TABLE worklist (item_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, claimed_by TEXT, claimed_at TEXT, released_at TEXT, status TEXT). Use SQLite's BEGIN EXCLUSIVE transaction around INSERT OR IGNORE + SELECT to implement atomic_claim(item_id, agent_id) -> bool. Add release(item_id) and reap_stale(timeout_s) helpers. Expose via WorklistManager class.
test_plan: Spin 10 concurrent asyncio tasks each calling atomic_claim on the same item_id; assert exactly 1 returns True. Test reap_stale by inserting a row with claimed_at 10 minutes ago and verifying it is released.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py — no Tool named 'kubectl_exec' appears anywhere in the file; grep of src/ for 'enhanced_tools' returns zero results in mcp_server.py
diagnosis: The schema for kubectl_exec does not exist in enhanced_tools.py and the module is never imported by mcp_server.py, so there is no kubectl exec capability exposed to MCP clients.
proposed_fix: Add Tool('kubectl_exec', ...) to get_enhanced_kubectl_tools() with required fields pod_name, command (list), namespace, container (optional). Add 'from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools' to mcp_server.py and extend handle_list_tools to include these tools. Add dispatch case in handle_call_tool routing to kubectl_manager.exec_command().
test_plan: After wiring, call the tool via MCP with a real pod name and verify the kubectl_manager.exec_command method is invoked; confirm the response contains stdout/stderr fields, not a 'Unknown tool' error.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:560-563 — delegates to documentation_manager.search_documentation(); src/documentation_manager.py:328-373 — searches self.documentation_db which is populated at init by live HTTP crawls to kubernetes.io
diagnosis: The tool calls real HTTP scraping code, but on startup failure (network unavailable, rate-limit, or parsing error) documentation_db stays empty and the method silently returns [] with no indication of the failure source.
proposed_fix: In search_documentation, if len(self.documentation_db) == 0, return a structured error dict {'error': 'documentation_db empty', 'cause': 'init crawl may have failed'} instead of []. In _build_documentation_index, capture and surface aggregate crawl errors. Add a last_indexed_at timestamp and expose it so callers can tell if data is stale.
test_plan: Mock aiohttp to raise ConnectionError during init; call search_documentation and assert the response contains an 'error' key. Then provide a mock successful crawl and verify results are returned.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:504 — Tool named 'uninstall_helm_chart' exists in get_enhanced_helm_tools(); grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' returns zero results — module is never imported
diagnosis: The uninstall_helm_chart schema exists in enhanced_tools.py but mcp_server.py never imports that module, so the tool is invisible to MCP clients and calls to it return 'Unknown tool'.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py add: from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_helm_tools. Extend handle_list_tools to include get_enhanced_helm_tools() in the returned list. Add elif name == 'uninstall_helm_chart': dispatch to self.helm_manager.uninstall(...) in handle_call_tool.
test_plan: After wiring, list tools via MCP and assert 'uninstall_helm_chart' appears. Call the tool with a dummy release_name and verify helm_manager.uninstall is invoked, not the fallback 'Unknown tool' branch.

evidence: k8s/service.yaml:37-47 — NodePort service with hardcoded nodePort: 30001 exposes the MCP stdio server externally; no NetworkPolicy restricts which pods may reach it
diagnosis: The NodePort service creates a cluster-wide external entry point on port 30001 with no NetworkPolicy restricting access, violating least-privilege network segmentation; the service also lacks an appProtocol field on its port definitions.
proposed_fix: Remove the NodePort service entirely unless external access is documented as a requirement; prefer an Ingress or internal ClusterIP. If NodePort is retained, add a NetworkPolicy: ingress from specific namespace/podSelector. Add appProtocol: http to port specs. Document the reason for nodePort: 30001 being hardcoded.
