evidence: N/A — design task; no dedup logic exists anywhere in src/
diagnosis: No finding deduplication exists; the auto-remediator would emit duplicate work orders for the same root cause (e.g., missing resource limits on 100 pods each flagged individually).
proposed_fix: Data model: Finding(root_cause_key: str, severity: str, affected_resources: List[str], first_seen: datetime, count: int). root_cause_key = sha256(finding_type + namespace + container_image_or_role). Interface: dedupe(findings: List[RawFinding]) -> List[Finding]. Algorithm: group by root_cause_key, merge affected_resources, keep highest severity, set count. Store in a dict keyed by root_cause_key; on new scan, upsert. Edge cases: different namespaces with same root cause should remain separate keys (include namespace in key).
test_plan: Feed 50 raw findings all with type=missing_resource_limits across 50 pods; assert dedupe() returns exactly 1 Finding with count=50 and affected_resources length 50; feed 2 findings with different namespaces and same type; assert 2 Findings are returned.

evidence: N/A — design task; src/security_manager.py and src/mcp_server.py have no structured audit log, only unstructured logger.info calls
diagnosis: There is no immutable audit log recording who triggered each remediation action, what was changed, when, and what evidence justified it — required for compliance and post-incident review.
proposed_fix: Data model: AuditEntry(id: uuid, timestamp: ISO8601, actor: str, action: str, target_resource: str, namespace: str, finding_id: str, dry_run: bool, outcome: str, evidence: str). Interface: audit_log.append(entry: AuditEntry). Storage: append-only JSONL file at /data/audit.log (the PVC mount). Wire into execute_remediation in mcp_server.py as a post-action decorator: after every tool call that mutates cluster state, create and append an AuditEntry. Edge cases: dry_run=True entries must still be logged with outcome='dry_run'.
test_plan: Execute two remediations (one dry_run=True, one live) and assert /data/audit.log contains exactly two entries with correct actor, action, and dry_run fields; assert appending never truncates existing entries.

evidence: N/A — design task; k8s/pvc.yaml and k8s/ manifests show a single ServiceAccount with no least-privilege splitting
diagnosis: The server uses a single identity for both read-only diagnosis and write operations, violating least-privilege — a bug in the write path could gain cluster-wide read access or vice versa.
proposed_fix: Create two ServiceAccounts: 'mcp-reader' (ClusterRole: get/list/watch on pods, events, nodes, services, deployments, replicasets, statefulsets, daemonsets, configmaps, networkpolicies, roles, rolebindings) and 'mcp-writer' (Role scoped per namespace: patch deployments, create/delete pods). KubernetesManager accepts a read_client and write_client. Diagnosis methods use read_client; execute_remediation uses write_client. Wire via projected ServiceAccount tokens or two kubeconfig contexts.
test_plan: With only read_client, attempt to patch a deployment and assert AuthorizationError is raised. With write_client, attempt to list cluster nodes and assert the call is rejected by RBAC. Integration test using two kind service account tokens.

evidence: N/A — design task; no DeploymentAnalyzer class exists in src/
diagnosis: No automated analysis logic exists to detect replica mismatches (spec.replicas != status.readyReplicas) or stuck rollouts (Progressing condition with reason=ProgressDeadlineExceeded).
proposed_fix: Interface: DeploymentAnalyzer.analyze(deployment: V1Deployment) -> List[Finding]. Logic: (1) replica_mismatch: if deployment.status.ready_replicas != deployment.spec.replicas, emit Finding(type='replica_mismatch', severity='high', evidence=f'ready={ready} spec={spec}'). (2) stuck_rollout: iterate deployment.status.conditions; if type=='Progressing' and reason=='ProgressDeadlineExceeded', emit Finding(type='stuck_rollout', severity='critical'). Register in DiagnosticsManager.diagnose_cluster_health under check_types=['deployments'].
test_plan: Mock a V1Deployment with ready_replicas=1, spec.replicas=3; assert analyze() returns one finding with type=replica_mismatch. Mock a deployment with ProgressDeadlineExceeded condition; assert analyze() returns stuck_rollout finding with severity=critical.

evidence: k8s/pvc.yaml:1-16 — entire file is a bare PVC spec with no annotations, no encryption-at-rest label, storageClassName: local-path (line 15), and accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce] (line 9); no volumeMode specified
diagnosis: The PVC lacks volumeMode: Filesystem (should be explicit), has no encryption-at-rest annotation, and uses storageClassName: local-path which is node-local and unsuitable for production HA deployments; audit log data stored here would be lost on node failure.
proposed_fix: Add 'volumeMode: Filesystem' under spec. Add annotation 'encryption: required' or document the expectation that the storage class encrypts at rest. Replace storageClassName: local-path with a parameterized value (e.g., via Helm values or a comment noting production requires a replicated storage class). Add a comment citing the single-node limitation.
test_plan: Deploy to a multi-node cluster; confirm PVC binds on the correct node and data persists across pod restarts; confirm the volumeMode field appears in 'kubectl get pvc -o yaml'.

evidence: N/A — design task; no CronJobAnalyzer exists; the manual-cw5-003 reference implies orphaned jobs were found manually
diagnosis: No automated CronJob analysis detects missed schedules or orphaned Job objects left by failed CronJob runs, requiring manual triage as seen with manual-cw5-003.
proposed_fix: Interface: CronJobAnalyzer.analyze(cronjob: V1CronJob, jobs: List[V1Job]) -> List[Finding]. Logic: (1) missed_schedule: compare cronjob.status.last_schedule_time to expected next run using croniter; if overdue by more than one interval, emit Finding(type='missed_schedule'). (2) orphaned_jobs: for each Job where job.metadata.owner_references is empty and job.metadata.labels['cronjob-name'] matches, emit Finding(type='orphaned_job', severity='low'). Integrate into DiagnosticsManager.diagnose_cluster_health with check_types=['cronjobs'].
test_plan: Mock a CronJob with last_schedule_time 3 hours ago and schedule='*/30 * * * *'; assert analyze() returns missed_schedule. Create a Job with no ownerReferences but cronjob label; assert analyze() returns orphaned_job.

evidence: security-scan-demo.sh:1-3 — shebang is '#!/bin/bash' with no 'set -euo pipefail'; lines 14-18 use 'kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq ...' with no error handling if kubectl or jq are absent; line 8 uses 'kubectl config current-context' which exits non-zero if no context is set but the script continues
diagnosis: The script has no error-safety flags (set -euo pipefail), so failures in kubectl or jq silently produce empty variables that are then interpolated into the summary counts (lines 118-121), giving a false 'all clear' when tools are missing or the cluster is unreachable.
proposed_fix: Add 'set -euo pipefail' at line 2. Add dependency checks at top: 'command -v kubectl >/dev/null || { echo "kubectl not found"; exit 1; }' and same for jq. Wrap the 'kubectl config current-context' call with '|| { echo "No kubectl context"; exit 1; }'. Replace all empty-string checks with explicit exit-code checks on kubectl subcommands.
test_plan: Run the script with kubectl pointing to a non-existent cluster; assert the script exits non-zero with a meaningful error message rather than printing '0 detected' for all findings.

evidence: src/helm_manager.py:45 — '_run_helm' is a synchronous 'def' (blocking subprocess.run); lines 102-409 — every public method is declared 'async def' but calls 'return self._run_helm(args)' directly without 'await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(...)'; also line 301 — password is appended to CLI args list and logged at line 58 via 'Running helm command: ...' leaking credentials to logs; line 333-334 — max_results maps to --max-col-width (a display flag, not a result-count limit)
diagnosis: All async Helm methods block the asyncio event loop because _run_helm runs subprocess.run synchronously; additionally, repo passwords are leaked to logs, and max_results is wired to the wrong helm flag.
proposed_fix: Change _run_helm to async and use 'await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, functools.partial(subprocess.run, ...))'. Remove password from the logged command string (mask with '***'). For search_helm_charts, replace '--max-col-width' with '--max' to correctly limit result count. Also note: helm install/upgrade are not registered in mcp_server.py handle_call_tool — wire those elif branches.
test_plan: Run the event loop under a 1-second timeout and call list_helm_releases; assert the loop is not blocked (use asyncio.wait_for). Check server logs after add_helm_repository with a password; assert '***' appears instead of the real password. Call search_helm_charts with max_results=3 and assert at most 3 rows are returned.

evidence: helm_manager.py:58 — `logger.info(f"Running helm command: {' '.join(cmd)}")`; combined with helm_manager.py:296-301 where `--password` arg is appended to `args` and `cmd = ['helm'] + args`
diagnosis: Repository passwords passed to `add_helm_repository` are logged in plaintext via the INFO-level log statement that reconstructs the full command string.
proposed_fix: Scrub credential arguments before logging. After building `cmd`, create a sanitized copy: `log_cmd = [('***' if args[i-1] in ('--password', '--username') else a) for i, a in enumerate(cmd)]` and use `log_cmd` in the logger call only. Alternatively, log only the subcommand name (e.g., `helm repo add <name>`) without arguments.
test_plan: Unit-test `_run_helm(['repo', 'add', 'myrepo', 'https://...', '--username', 'u', '--password', 'secret'])` with a captured log handler and assert the string 'secret' does not appear in any log record.

evidence: helm_manager.py:125 — `repo_result = await self.add_helm_repository(repository, repository)` inside `install_helm_chart`
diagnosis: `install_helm_chart` passes the `repository` URL as both the repo `name` and `url` arguments, which would register a repo named after its full URL (e.g., `https://charts.helm.sh/stable`) — an invalid Helm repo name that will always fail.
proposed_fix: Either require a separate `repository_name` parameter in `install_helm_chart`, or derive a safe slug from the URL: `repo_name = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]', '-', url.rstrip('/').rsplit('/', 1)[-1].lower())`. Pass that slug as `name` to `add_helm_repository`.
test_plan: Call `install_helm_chart('rel', 'stable/nginx', repository='https://charts.helm.sh/stable')` with a mock `_run_helm` and assert the `helm repo add` call receives a valid name (no slashes/colons), not the raw URL.

evidence: documentation_manager.py:40-49 — `async def initialize(self): ... await self._build_documentation_index()` which makes live HTTP requests to `kubernetes.io` at server startup
diagnosis: `DocumentationManager.initialize()` performs uncached live web-scraping of kubernetes.io at every server start; any network failure, rate-limit, or timeout causes the entire MCP server startup to raise and abort, and the scraped data is only in-memory (lost on restart).
proposed_fix: Wrap `_build_documentation_index` in a try/except and degrade gracefully (log warning, leave `documentation_db` empty) rather than raising. Add a file-based or SQLite cache keyed on URL hash with a configurable TTL (e.g., 24 h) so restarts reuse persisted content. Remove the `raise` at line 49.
test_plan: Mock `aiohttp.ClientSession.get` to raise `aiohttp.ClientError`; assert `initialize()` completes without exception, `documentation_db` is empty, and a warning is logged. Also test that a second call within TTL reads from cache and issues zero HTTP requests.

evidence: documentation_manager.py:280-326 — `async def _load_best_practices(self)` sets `self.best_practices` to a hardcoded dict of static strings with no external source
diagnosis: `_load_best_practices` is declared `async` and appears to load data, but it only assigns a hardcoded in-memory dict; the `async` keyword is misleading and the data is never refreshed.
proposed_fix: Either rename to `_init_best_practices` and make it synchronous (removing the `async` keyword), or replace with an actual load from a bundled YAML/JSON file (`best_practices.yaml`) so content can be updated without code changes. Add a comment clarifying this is static seed data.
test_plan: Assert `_load_best_practices` is idempotent (calling twice does not duplicate entries), and verify all six category keys are present with at least one item each.

evidence: mcp_server.py:560-563 — handler calls `self.documentation_manager.search_documentation(...)`; documentation_manager.py:328-370 — method iterates `self.documentation_db` which is populated only by live HTTP scraping at startup
diagnosis: `search_documentation` searches an in-memory dict populated by scraping kubernetes.io at startup; if startup scraping fails or has not yet completed, `documentation_db` is empty and the tool silently returns an empty list rather than an error.
proposed_fix: In `search_documentation`, check `if not self.documentation_db` and return a structured error response `{'error': 'Documentation index not yet built', 'fallback': self.best_practices}` instead of an empty list. Also surface a readiness flag via a `is_ready()` method that mcp_server can check before dispatching.
test_plan: Call `search_documentation('pod')` on a `DocumentationManager` whose `documentation_db` is empty; assert the result contains an `error` key and the best_practices fallback is included.

evidence: setup-vscode-k8s.sh:77,89 — hardcoded path `/Users/hawaiidevelopergmail.com/Library/Application Support/Code/User/settings.json` (missing the `@` separator between user and domain in the home directory path)
diagnosis: The embedded Python heredoc uses a hardcoded, malformed absolute path (`hawaiidevelopergmail.com` instead of `hawaiideveloper`) which will raise `FileNotFoundError` on any machine including the author's, making the script completely non-functional.
proposed_fix: Replace both hardcoded paths with the shell variable `$SETTINGS_FILE` that is correctly set on line 9 using `$HOME`. Pass `$SETTINGS_FILE` into the Python heredoc as an environment variable or argument: `python3 -c "import os,json; ..." "$SETTINGS_FILE"`, avoiding heredoc variable quoting issues.
test_plan: Run `bash -n setup-vscode-k8s.sh` to check syntax; then run with `SETTINGS_FILE=/tmp/test_settings.json` (pre-populated with `{}`) and verify the file is updated with the MCP config keys and the original content is preserved.

evidence: stop.sh:1-5 — no `set -euo pipefail`; stops `docker stop k8s-mcp-server` but server is described throughout docs as running via `kubectl` / stdio, not as a named Docker container
diagnosis: `stop.sh` targets a Docker container named `k8s-mcp-server` with no `set -euo pipefail`, but the server is a stdio MCP process launched by an MCP host (not a standalone Docker container), so this script is a no-op in the documented deployment model.
proposed_fix: Add `set -euo pipefail` at line 1. Rewrite to kill the actual process: `pkill -f 'python.*mcp_server.py' || true`. If Docker is a supported deployment mode, document it explicitly and add a mode flag (`--docker` vs `--process`). Remove the misleading `|| true` silencer on the `docker rm` which hides real errors.
test_plan: Verify `bash -n stop.sh` passes. Run in a environment where `python mcp_server.py` is backgrounded; assert the process is no longer running after `stop.sh` completes. Confirm script exits non-zero when invoked with `set -e` and the target does not exist (i.e., the `|| true` is intentional and documented).

evidence: No PVCAnalyzer class exists anywhere in src/; closest existing code is k8s_manager.py (not read in this kit) which handles generic cluster info
diagnosis: PVCAnalyzer for pending/mount-failed/capacity states is a missing feature that needs a concrete design before implementation.
proposed_fix: Data model: `PVCFinding(name, namespace, phase, capacity_bytes, used_bytes, storage_class, bound_pv, events: List[str], diagnosis: str, remediation: str)`. Interface: `async def analyze_pvc(name: str, namespace: str) -> PVCFinding`. Dependencies: `kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api.read_namespaced_persistent_volume_claim`, `list_namespaced_event` filtered by `involvedObject.name`. Logic branches: (1) phase==Pending -> inspect StorageClass provisioner, check PV availability, surface top event; (2) mount-failed -> parse pod events for `FailedMount`, extract node and volume plugin error; (3) capacity -> compare `status.capacity` vs metrics-server usage, flag >80% threshold. Edge cases: RWO PVC bound to unavailable node, dynamic provisioner timeout, CSI driver not installed.
test_plan: Unit-test each branch with mocked CoreV1Api responses: assert Pending returns a diagnosis mentioning the provisioner name; assert mount-failed surfaces the node name from events; assert capacity >80% sets a warning flag. Integration-test against a kind cluster with a PVC in Pending state.

evidence: test_security_compliance.py:127-130 — `security_manager.resolve_privileged_pod_violation = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resolution)` then immediately `await security_manager.resolve_privileged_pod_violation(security_context)`; identical pattern at lines 255, 377, 505, 651, 763, 898
diagnosis: Every test installs its own AsyncMock as the method under test and then calls that mock — the tests assert only that the mock returns what it was told to return, providing zero coverage of real SecurityManager behavior.
proposed_fix: Remove the `security_manager.<method> = AsyncMock(...)` assignment lines. Implement the actual methods in SecurityManager (calling the kubernetes client), then mock only the underlying k8s API calls (e.g., `manager.k8s_client.list_namespaced_pod = AsyncMock(...)`). Assertions should then validate real logic: field extraction, severity mapping, remediation YAML generation.
test_plan: After rewrite, run the test suite with coverage; assert that `SecurityManager.resolve_privileged_pod_violation` (the real method, not a mock) is covered. Confirm tests fail when the real method is changed to return incorrect severity values.

evidence: k8s/rbac.yaml:20 — `resources: ["nodes", "namespaces", "pods", "services", "endpoints", "persistentvolumes", "persistentvolumeclaims", "configmaps", "secrets", "events"]` all granted `get, list, watch` in a single ClusterRole
diagnosis: The ClusterRole grants read access to `secrets` cluster-wide; for an MCP diagnostic server that only needs to read pod/node/event data, this grants unnecessary access to all secret values in every namespace, violating least-privilege.
proposed_fix: Remove `secrets` from the broad `get/list/watch` ClusterRole rule. Add a separate, namespace-scoped Role in `mcp-kubernetes` only if the server needs its own operational secrets (e.g., kubeconfig). If secret scanning is required, scope it to a dedicated audit Role with `list` only (no `get`) and bind it to a separate ServiceAccount used only for that scan operation.
test_plan: Apply the modified `rbac.yaml` to a test cluster; run `kubectl auth can-i get secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:mcp-kubernetes:kubernetes-mcp-server-sa -n kube-system` and confirm it returns `no`. Verify existing diagnostic tools still function by running the MCP server's `diagnose_cluster_health` tool against a live cluster.

evidence: github_issues_manager.py:573-618 — get_issue_statistics() queries a SQLite DB populated by real GitHub API calls via aiohttp; mcp_server.py:605-606 dispatches to it correctly
diagnosis: The handler exists and queries real data from a SQLite database, but returns empty dict on any error (line 617) with no indication to the caller that data may be absent or stale due to failed GitHub fetches.
proposed_fix:
1. Return a structured error envelope instead of `{}` on exception.
2. Add a `last_fetched_at` and `fetch_status` field to the stats dict so the caller can tell whether the DB is populated.
3. On empty DB (total_issues == 0) return a sentinel `{"status": "empty", "reason": "db_not_yet_populated"}`.
test_plan: Unit-test get_issue_statistics() against an empty DB and assert a non-empty error envelope is returned. Integration-test by mocking the GitHub API to fail and verifying the stats endpoint still returns a parseable dict with an error flag.

evidence: No HPAAnalyzer class exists anywhere in src/; monitoring_manager.py and diagnostics_manager.py both contain hardcoded/canned data. The README.md line 151 claims 'Auto-scaling Ready - Deployment configured for horizontal pod autoscaling' without any HPA analysis implementation.
diagnosis: There is no HPA analyzer anywhere in the codebase; the feature is entirely unimplemented despite the README advertising it.
proposed_fix:
Create src/hpa_analyzer.py with class HPAAnalyzer:
  async def analyze(self, namespace: Optional[str]) -> Dict:
    hpas = k8s_client.AutoscalingV2Api().list_namespaced_hpa(ns)
    for hpa in hpas:
      current = hpa.status.current_metrics  # may be None if metrics-server absent
      desired = hpa.status.desired_replicas
      max_r = hpa.spec.max_replicas
      yield findings: [at_max, no_metrics, thrashing]
Edge cases: metrics-server not installed (current_metrics is None), HPA targeting non-existent workload, minReplicas==maxReplicas.
test_plan: Unit-test with mocked AutoscalingV2Api responses covering: (a) HPA at max replicas, (b) HPA with nil current_metrics, (c) HPA with scale-down thrashing (desired fluctuating). Integration-test against a real cluster with a synthetic HPA and verify findings are populated.

evidence: mcp_server.py:571-575 — dispatches to documentation_manager.find_commands(); documentation_manager.py:382-399 — find_commands() iterates self.documentation_db which is populated by scraping kubernetes.io at startup via aiohttp; if the scrape fails (network unavailable, rate-limited, HTML structure changed), documentation_db remains empty and the method silently returns [].
diagnosis: find_kubectl_commands returns silently empty results whenever the runtime web-scrape of kubernetes.io fails, with no fallback static command reference and no error surfaced to the caller.
proposed_fix:
Bundle a minimal static kubectl command reference JSON file in the repo (e.g. src/data/kubectl_commands.json).
In find_commands(), check if documentation_db is empty and fall back to loading from the static file.
If both are empty, return a structured error: {"error": "documentation_index_not_loaded", "commands": []}.
test_plan: Unit-test find_commands() with documentation_db empty and assert the static fallback is used. Integration-test that after a simulated scrape failure the tool still returns a non-empty list of common kubectl commands.

evidence: functional_unit_test.md:66-86 — test_crashloopbackoff_pattern_recognition calls manager.analyze_issue_pattern(pattern); github_issues_manager.py has no method analyze_issue_pattern (confirmed via grep of all `def ` in that file). Also line 79: asserts result["solutions"], result["sub_component"] — none of these keys exist in the DB schema (github_issues table has no solutions column).
diagnosis: The functional test document describes 390 tests that call methods and assert on data structures that do not exist in the actual codebase, making every documented test non-executable fiction.
proposed_fix: Either (a) implement the missing methods (analyze_issue_pattern, etc.) and DB schema changes to make the tests runnable, or (b) replace the document with tests that target real methods: get_issue_statistics(), find_similar_issues(), search_issues(). Mark all tests that call non-existent methods with a TODO comment and a GitHub issue reference so they are not mistaken for passing tests.
test_plan: Run `pytest tests/ -v` and verify zero import errors or AttributeError. All documented test signatures should correspond to real callable methods.

evidence: README.md:58 — 'Production Ready: Successfully deployed ... 1,029+ official Kubernetes reference files'; README.md:204-205 — '45,720+ indexed issues'; README.md:477-480 — example /stats output shows {"total_issues": 47832, ...}. The documentation_db is built at runtime by scraping kubernetes.io (documentation_manager.py:51-66); the github_issues DB is populated by live GitHub API calls (github_issues_manager.py:145-166). Neither dataset is bundled; both may be empty on a fresh start with no network.
diagnosis: The README makes concrete production-readiness and scale claims (45,720+ issues, 1,029+ docs, live health endpoint) that are contingent on successful runtime network operations that may not complete, and advertises an HTTP /health endpoint that does not exist in the MCP stdio server.
proposed_fix:
1. Replace concrete counts with 'up to N issues fetched at runtime'.
2. Add a caveat section: 'Knowledge base is empty on first start until GitHub API and docs scrape complete (may take minutes; requires network and optionally GITHUB_TOKEN)'.
3. Remove the /health and /stats curl examples — mcp_server.py serves stdio only, not HTTP.
4. Remove the claim '172.100.10.107:30001 Currently Operational' (line 1101) — this is a development environment address not relevant to users.
test_plan: Review README against code: grep for every curl example and confirm a matching HTTP handler exists in src/. Confirm all feature bullet points map to callable MCP tool methods.

evidence: mcp_server.py:577-579 — dispatches to documentation_manager.get_troubleshooting_guide(issue_type); documentation_manager.py:401-414 — iterates self.documentation_db and matches on trouble['title'] or trouble['content']; if documentation_db is empty (scrape failed), returns [].
diagnosis: get_troubleshooting_guide silently returns an empty list when the runtime docs scrape of kubernetes.io has not completed or failed, providing no guidance to the caller.
proposed_fix:
1. Bundle a minimal static troubleshooting guide dict keyed by common issue types (pod-crash, image-pull, network, storage, rbac) in src/data/troubleshooting_guides.json.
2. In get_troubleshooting_guide(), if the scraped DB yields no results, fall back to the static guides with a 'source: static' flag.
3. Return {"error": "no_guides_found", "issue_type": issue_type} instead of [] when both sources are empty.
test_plan: Unit-test with documentation_db empty and assert static fallback guides are returned for known issue types. Test with an unknown issue_type and assert a structured error dict is returned instead of [].

evidence: status.sh:1 — no shebang guard; status.sh:1-12 — no `set -euo pipefail`; status.sh:4 — hardcodes `echo '✓ Health: OK (Docker healthcheck passed)'` unconditionally even when the container health state is not checked; the actual Docker health status is never queried.
diagnosis: status.sh hardcodes a 'Health: OK' success message regardless of actual container health, providing false assurance, and lacks error-exit discipline.
proposed_fix:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
if docker ps --filter 'name=k8s-mcp-server' --filter 'status=running' --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -q k8s-mcp-server; then
  health=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' k8s-mcp-server 2>/dev/null || echo 'none')
  echo "Container: Running | Health: ${health}"
else
  echo 'Container: Not running'; exit 1
fi
test_plan: Run status.sh with a stopped container and confirm exit code 1. Run with a running but unhealthy container (docker inspect returns 'unhealthy') and confirm that status is printed accurately.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py — no Tool named 'kubectl_exec' appears anywhere in the file (grep confirms only 'kubectl_generic' at line 309-323); mcp_server.py — no import of enhanced_tools (grep returns no output); the entire enhanced_tools.py module is unreachable from the running server.
diagnosis: kubectl_exec does not exist as a schema in enhanced_tools.py, and even if it did, the module is not imported by mcp_server.py so no enhanced tool schema is ever registered.
proposed_fix:
1. Add Tool('kubectl_exec', ...) schema to enhanced_tools.py with fields: pod_name (required), namespace, container, command (required), stdin (bool).
2. Implement the handler in kubectl_manager.py using subprocess(['kubectl','exec','-n',ns,pod,'--',*cmd]) with timeout and output size limits.
3. Wire up: in mcp_server.py add `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools` and include in ListToolsResult; add elif name=='kubectl_exec' dispatch branch.
4. In non_destructive_mode, block if command contains write-side-effects.
test_plan: Unit-test that kubectl_exec is present in the ListToolsResult. Integration-test against a real pod running 'echo hello' and verify stdout is returned. Test that non_destructive_mode blocks commands like 'rm -rf'.

evidence: mcp_server.py:593-596 — dispatches to github_issues_manager.find_similar_issues(); github_issues_manager.py:488-489 — builds SQL with string interpolation: `f"title LIKE '%{term}%' OR body LIKE '%{term}%'"` for each key_term without parameterization; this is executed directly via aiosqlite.connect().execute(sql, ...) at line 500.
diagnosis: find_similar_issues constructs SQL by embedding user-derived terms directly into the query string, enabling SQL injection via crafted error_message inputs.
proposed_fix:
Replace the f-string SQL construction with parameterized queries:
conditions = ' OR '.join(['(title LIKE ? OR body LIKE ?)'] * len(key_terms))
params = [val for t in key_terms for val in (f'%{t}%', f'%{t}%')]
params.append(max_results)
sql = f'SELECT ... FROM github_issues WHERE ({conditions}) AND state = ? ORDER BY ... LIMIT ?'
params.insert(-1, 'closed')
Then execute(sql, params).
test_plan: Pass error_message="' OR '1'='1" and verify no unexpected rows are returned and no SQLite error is raised. Add a test with a normal error message and verify results are returned correctly.

evidence: start.sh:8 — uses `set -e` only (no `-u` or `-o pipefail`); start.sh:97-103 — GITHUB_TOKEN printed directly into docker run ENV_ARGS: `ENV_ARGS="$ENV_ARGS -e GITHUB_TOKEN=$GITHUB_TOKEN"` — the token value is visible in `docker inspect` and `ps aux` output on the host; start.sh:193-231 — the fallback jq-not-found branch at line 233 replaces the entire VS Code settings.json with only the MCP config, silently destroying user settings.
diagnosis: start.sh leaks the GITHUB_TOKEN into Docker environment args visible in process listings, and unconditionally overwrites VS Code user settings when jq is absent.
proposed_fix:
1. Add `set -euo pipefail` at line 8.
2. Pass token via --env-file: write token to a temp file with `mktemp`, chmod 600, use `--env-file` flag, then `rm` the temp file after docker run.
3. In the jq-absent branch: refuse to proceed and print an error 'jq is required for safe settings merge; install with: brew install jq' instead of overwriting settings.
test_plan: Run start.sh with a dummy GITHUB_TOKEN; verify `docker inspect k8s-mcp-server` does not expose the token value in Env[]. Run without jq and verify the script exits non-zero and settings.json is untouched.

evidence: health_check.py:38-46 — check_imports() validates only stdlib modules (asyncio, json, logging); health_check.py:49-55 — check_file_permissions() only verifies the app directory exists as a dir; no check that kubernetes client, mcp, or any real dependency is importable or reachable
diagnosis: The health check tests only Python built-in availability and directory existence, never verifying that the kubernetes client, MCP library, or kubeconfig is present and valid, so it will return True even when the server cannot connect to any cluster.
proposed_fix: Add check_kubernetes_client() that does `from kubernetes import client, config; config.load_incluster_config()` or `config.load_kube_config()` and catches exceptions. Add check_mcp_import() that imports `mcp.server`. Wire both into check(). Return False with a descriptive message on any failure rather than silently swallowing exceptions at line 29.
test_plan: Run health_check.py with KUBECONFIG unset — should return False. Run with a valid kubeconfig to a live cluster — should return True. Inject an ImportError for the kubernetes package and confirm False is returned.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:532-537 — dispatches to self.security_manager.security_scan(); src/security_manager.py:92-116 (_scan_rbac), 118-150 (_scan_pod_security), 152-176 (_scan_network_policies), 178-201 (_scan_secrets), 203-232 (_scan_container_images) — every private scan method returns a hardcoded dict literal with fabricated counts, CVEs, and percentages; no kubernetes client is imported in security_manager.py
diagnosis: The security_scan tool returns entirely fabricated data (e.g., hardcoded 'total_pods: 45', 'overall_security_score: 85', CVE 'nginx:1.18') from stub methods that never call the Kubernetes API, making every scan result false regardless of actual cluster state.
proposed_fix: In SecurityManager.__init__, accept a kubernetes CoreV1Api / RbacAuthorizationV1Api client. In _scan_rbac, call rbac_v1.list_cluster_role_binding() and rbac_v1.list_role_binding_for_all_namespaces(). In _scan_pod_security, call core_v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces() and inspect spec.securityContext. In _scan_secrets, call core_v1.list_secret_for_all_namespaces(). Return real counts. Remove all hardcoded dicts.
test_plan: Mock kubernetes client responses with known fixtures (e.g., one privileged pod, one wildcard role), call security_scan(['pods', 'rbac']), assert returned issues match exactly the fixture data. Run against a test cluster with no issues and assert issues_found is empty.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:106-128 — kubectl_delete Tool schema defined; grep confirms 'enhanced_tools' is never imported in src/mcp_server.py (grep returned no output); src/mcp_server.py handle_call_tool (lines 483-621) contains no 'kubectl_delete' branch
diagnosis: The kubectl_delete tool schema is declared in enhanced_tools.py and the handler exists in kubectl_manager.py:152, but mcp_server.py never imports enhanced_tools nor includes a dispatch branch for kubectl_delete, so the tool is completely dead and unreachable by any MCP client.
proposed_fix: In mcp_server.py, add `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools` and append the returned tools to the list_tools response. Add an elif branch in handle_call_tool: `elif name == 'kubectl_delete': result = await self.kubectl_manager.kubectl_delete(resource=arguments['resource'], name=arguments['name'], namespace=arguments.get('namespace', 'default'))`. Apply same pattern to all other enhanced kubectl/helm tools.
test_plan: Call the MCP list_tools endpoint and assert kubectl_delete appears. Call kubectl_delete with a test resource and confirm the kubectl_manager.kubectl_delete method is invoked. In non-destructive mode, confirm the tool returns a disabled message rather than executing.

evidence: No state machine exists in the codebase; no iteration loop, stop condition, or persistent state store is implemented anywhere in src/
diagnosis: There is no implementation or design for a review-PRD-split-implement-verify loop with stop conditions; this behavior must be fully designed for the auto-remediator PRD.
proposed_fix: Data model: IterationState(id: UUID, phase: Enum[REVIEW|PRD|SPLIT|IMPLEMENT|VERIFY], component_id: str, findings: List[Finding], attempt: int, max_attempts: int=3, status: Enum[PENDING|RUNNING|DONE|FAILED]). Interface: async def advance(state: IterationState) -> IterationState. Dependencies: persistent store (sqlite or redis), per-phase handler registry. Stop conditions: status==DONE when verify passes; status==FAILED after max_attempts; loop aborts if phase==VERIFY and all assertions pass. Edge cases: idempotent re-entry on crash recovery; timeout per phase (default 300s); human-approval gate before IMPLEMENT if dry_run=False.
test_plan: Unit-test each phase transition with mock handlers. Integration-test the full loop on a synthetic Finding that fails VERIFY once then passes on retry. Confirm FAILED state is set after max_attempts are exhausted. Confirm the loop does not advance past SPLIT without a non-empty PRD artifact.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:515-521 — dispatches to diagnostics_manager.check_network_connectivity(); src/diagnostics_manager.py:185-210 — comment reads '# Stub implementation for different test types' and every branch returns a hardcoded dict (e.g., latency_ms: 2.3, resolved_ip: '10.96.0.1', 'Connection timeout') regardless of actual source_pod or target values
diagnosis: check_network_connectivity ignores the source_pod and target arguments entirely and returns canned latency and error data from a stub, so network diagnostics are always fabricated and misleading.
proposed_fix: Use kubernetes CoreV1Api to verify source_pod exists and is Running. For ping/tcp tests, execute `kubectl exec {source_pod} -- nc -zv {target} {port}` via the kubernetes exec API (stream=True). For dns, exec `nslookup {target}`. For http, exec `curl -sI {target}`. Parse actual exit codes and stdout. Return real latency from time.monotonic() brackets around each exec call.
test_plan: In a test cluster, assert a running pod can reach its own service (expect success). Assert a pod in a namespace with default-deny NetworkPolicy cannot reach an isolated target (expect failure). Confirm that passing a nonexistent pod_name returns an error dict rather than fabricated success.

evidence: security_manager.py:1-10 — imports are asyncio, json, typing, config, logger only; no kubernetes import present; security_manager.py:23-30 — initialize() logs two lines and does nothing; security_manager.py:251-272 — _check_compliance_status() returns hardcoded CIS score '82%', NIST scores, all fabricated; security_manager.py:274-309 — _generate_security_recommendations() returns a static list unrelated to any scan input
diagnosis: SecurityManager has no Kubernetes client, performs zero real API calls, and produces entirely hardcoded compliance scores and recommendations, making every security report a fiction regardless of actual cluster configuration.
proposed_fix: Add kubernetes_client parameter to __init__. In initialize(), call config.load_incluster_config() or config.load_kube_config() and instantiate CoreV1Api, RbacAuthorizationV1Api, NetworkingV1Api. Replace each _scan_* stub with real API calls. Derive _check_compliance_status() from aggregated real findings rather than returning constants. Make _generate_security_recommendations() filter from a rules table keyed on actual issue types found.
test_plan: Mock the kubernetes clients to return a cluster with one privileged pod; assert the scan returns exactly that one high-severity issue and overall_security_score is not 85. Assert that a cluster with no issues returns score 100 and empty issues_found list.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:489-492 — dispatches to k8s_manager.get_cluster_info(); src/k8s_manager.py:49-89 — comment at line 49 reads '# Stub implementation - in real implementation, this would query the cluster'; returns hardcoded version 'v1.29.0', nodes total:3, pods running:42, api_server 'https://kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local', network_plugin 'calico' — none from real API calls
diagnosis: get_cluster_info returns hardcoded cluster topology data that is always identical regardless of the actual connected cluster, so any consumer of this tool receives false information.
proposed_fix: In KubernetesManager.initialize(), load kubeconfig and instantiate CoreV1Api and VersionApi. In get_cluster_info(), call version_api.get_code() for version, core_v1.list_node() for node counts and status, core_v1.list_namespace() for namespace count, core_v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces() for pod phase counts, core_v1.list_service_for_all_namespaces() for service type counts. Remove all hardcoded literals.
test_plan: Mock VersionApi.get_code() to return v1.30.1 and list_node() to return 2 nodes (1 Ready, 1 NotReady); assert get_cluster_info returns version 'v1.30.1' and nodes.not_ready == 1. Integration test: run against a real single-node cluster and confirm the response reflects actual state.

evidence: TEST_SUITE_IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md:5 — claims '390 Total Tests'; actual test counts by file: test_pod_issues.py=10, test_network_service_issues.py=9, test_cluster_management.py=8, test_security_compliance.py=7, test_issue_patterns.py=21 — total=55 tests. Line 36 claims '5 Test Categories' (Unit, Integration, Performance, Security, Production) with directories integration/, performance/, security/ — none of these three directories exist (only unit/ and production/ exist per filesystem listing). Line 110 claims 'Full integration with Kubernetes client libraries' — security_manager and k8s_manager have zero kubernetes client calls.
diagnosis: The summary document overclaims 390 tests (actual: 55), fabricates three missing test category directories (integration/, performance/, security/), and falsely asserts full Kubernetes API integration that does not exist in any source file.
proposed_fix: Replace statistics table with accurate counts (55 tests across 2 categories). Remove claims about integration/, performance/, security/ directories until they are created. Remove the 'Full integration with Kubernetes client libraries' claim and replace with 'Managers currently use stub implementations; real API integration is pending.' Add a 'Known gaps' section listing the three missing test directories and the stub-only managers.
test_plan: After doc update, run `grep -c 'def test_' tests/**/*.py` and confirm the count matches what the doc states. CI lint step should diff claimed counts vs actual pytest --collect-only output.

evidence: k8s/service.yaml:1-48 — the file contains only two Service resources (ClusterIP and NodePort); no securityContext, no resource limits, no readiness/liveness probes, no NetworkPolicy, no RBAC objects. The NodePort service at line 28-48 exposes port 30001 externally with no authentication or network policy guard.
diagnosis: The service manifest lacks all security hardening: no NetworkPolicy to restrict ingress, no RBAC ServiceAccount binding, no resource quotas, and the NodePort exposure at 30001 is unrestricted, creating an unauthenticated external attack surface.
proposed_fix: Add a NetworkPolicy resource that allows ingress to port 3001 only from labeled MCP-client pods. Add a ServiceAccount + ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding granting only list/get/watch on pods, nodes, namespaces, secrets, services, networkpolicies. Consider removing or disabling the NodePort service in production and replacing with an Ingress with TLS termination. Document that both services share a namespace and that namespace should have a default-deny NetworkPolicy.
test_plan: Deploy the updated manifest to a test cluster; confirm a pod without the correct label cannot reach port 3001. Confirm the NodePort is absent or inaccessible from outside the cluster. Run `kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:mcp-kubernetes:mcp-server` and confirm it returns 'yes' only for the scoped resources.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:458-503 — upgrade_helm_chart Tool schema defined with name 'upgrade_helm_chart'; grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools', 'helm_upgrade', 'upgrade_helm_chart' returns no output — the module is never imported and no dispatch branch exists; src/helm_manager.py:141 — async def upgrade_helm_chart() implementation exists but is unreachable via MCP
diagnosis: The upgrade_helm_chart enhanced tool schema and the helm_manager.upgrade_helm_chart() implementation both exist but mcp_server.py never imports enhanced_tools and has no dispatch branch for 'upgrade_helm_chart', so Helm upgrade is completely inaccessible via the MCP protocol.

Add dispatch branch: `elif name == 'upgrade_helm_chart': result = await self.helm_manager.upgrade_helm_chart(release_name=arguments['release_name'], chart=arguments['chart'], namespace=arguments.get('namespace'), values=arguments.get('values'), chart_version=arguments.get('chart_version'), install=arguments.get('install', True), dry_run=arguments.get('dry_run', False))`. Apply the same wire-up to all other get_enhanced_helm_tools entries.
test_plan: After wire-up, call list_tools and confirm upgrade_helm_chart is present. Mock helm_manager.upgrade_helm_chart() to return a success dict; call the tool via MCP and assert the response matches. Confirm that dry_run=True invokes helm with --dry-run flag and returns the rendered manifest without applying it.
